EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was moving southeast from central Kentucky and Tennessee toward the southeast U.S.  Unstable northwest flow aloft was evident across the region.  Considerable dynamics and shear were noted with this system, but thermodynamics and instability were very limited as a very dry, stable air mass had resided over the area during previous days.  Widespread showers and thunderstorms were noted across the area throughout much of the day.  Isolated thunderstorms became strong to severe during the afternoon producing gusty winds and marginal severe hail.  Strong rotation in one storm even prompted a tornado warning during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Macon County Sheriff's office reported that four trees were blown down near Georgia Highway 224 between Shiloh Church Road and Mennonite School Road, just east-northeast of Montezuma.  A funnel cloud was also observed by the public in this area at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very wet fall and winter, rainfall during the March and April period fell below normal in many areas, especially the north part of the state.  Wildfires were on the increase during April 2010 with several human caused wildfires reported by the Georgia Forestry Commission during the month.  Wildfires were reported in Gilmer, Chattooga, Fannin, Union, and Murray counties.  The most significant wildfire during the month was actually the result of a T9 military aircraft crash in Fannin county just east of Blue Ridge.  This incident fueled a 23 acre wild fire in the forests just east of Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported a human-caused wildfire in the Haissiers Mill area (private land) burned six acres and resulted in roughly $2,000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the higher elevations of northeast Georgia overnight and into the morning of the 12th. By mid-morning, most areas above about 1500 feet had 1 to 4 inches of snow. Some areas above 3000 feet had as much as 6 inches. Lower elevations had up to an inch.

Widespread surface heating and continued deep moisture advection ahead of an approaching cold front from the northwest allowed for very unstable atmospheric conditions with CAPE values between 3500 to 4000 J/kg Friday afternoon and evening. As a 500 mb shortwave tracked along the southern periphery of a broad through and across the region, severe thunderstorms developed across the area. Severe thunderstorms initially developed along the seabreeze in the area late in the afternoon. More organized severe storms tracked into the area from the Midlands during the evening and pushed all the way into portions of southeast Georgia during the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down on a power line at the intersection of Oglethorpe Trail and Smith Lake Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was present over eastern Georgia during peak heating hours. An upper level trough began to round the southern periphery of a large longwave trough, thus forcing the front towards the East Coast during the evening. As this occurred, a few thunderstorms developed and resulted in marginal hail and strong downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported dime to penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing coastal storm system brought light to moderate snow to the mountains of northeast Georgia, and a mix of rain and snow to the foothills, starting late on Christmas morning, and continuing into the afternoon. By late afternoon, most locations enjoyed a rare white Christmas, with 1 to 4 inches of snow reported over the mountains, and around an inch over the foothills. Snow continued to fall steadily throughout Christmas evening, and by the time the snow tapered off early on the 26th, 5 to 7 inches of snow had fallen over the mountains, with 2 to 4 inches over the foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ten to eleven second period swells were present as distant tropical storm Colin made a north shift well offshore. Persistent northeast surface winds along with an afternoon sea breeze further enhanced the swell from Colin and and produced a strong wind wave component. As 15 mph winds developed along the afternoon sea breeze, several rip currents were reported along the beaches in southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tybee Island lifeguards reported 12 rescues and 14 assists due to strong rip currents during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary approached the Altamaha River basin during the mid afternoon from the north. A quasi-stationary front was draped northwest to southeast near the basin. In addition, the low level atmosphere was unstable with CAPEs over 2500 J/kg and lapse rates were decreasing aloft as an upper level short wave trough approached from the northwest. A few isolated severe storms developed near the Altamaha River basin and drifted southeast while producing large hail and wind damage. Storm total rainfall amounts neared 4 inches across portions of Jeff Davis and Appling counties. These storms interacted with the east coast sea breeze over Brantley, Pierce and Ware counties where cells began to rotate...and continued to produce large hail and damaging winds. Additional storms developed across Clinch and Echols counties in the evening where the east and west coast sea breezes combined with outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in a residential yard. Hail occurred, the size was unknown. The time of the event was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough persisted across the central U.S. with a subtropical ridge anchored over Georgia and the southeast U.S.  A nearly stationary front was draped across the Tennessee Valley into the southern Plains.  Ample moisture and instability were present for afternoon and evening thunderstorms, especially across north Georgia nearest the front.  Some of the storms became strong, producing heavy rain, small hail, and gusty winds, but none of the storms reached severe criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail in Dawsonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough continued to work slowly toward the southeast U.S. from the mid-south region.  High pressure aloft was weakening across the southeast U.S.  A slow moving frontal boundary had finally moved south to near the Tennessee-Georgia border.  Abundant moisture remained across the region.  Instability overcame the weak dynamics present across the region to allow for scattered to numerous afternoon showers and thunderstorms.  An isolated downburst from a small thunderstorm in Dooly county resulted in considerable wind damage to an recreational vehicle park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a thunderstorm downburst that resulted in minor damage to two recreational vehicles, a portion of a roof being blown off a building, five uprooted large pecan trees, and one downed power line.  Winds were estimated to be at least 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep upper low was moving through the mid-south and southeast U.S. from the 21st into the 22nd.  The associated surface low was suppressed very far south along the central and eastern Gulf Coast, with an occluded front stretching north from the surface low.  The occluded front moved across Georgia during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 21st.  The far suppression of the surface low and the associated deeper moisture greatly limited the severe threat with this system, although impressive shear and dynamics were noted.  A very narrow line of showers and a few thunderstorms accompanied the occluded front during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Gusty winds associated with this line of showers and thunderstorms blew down a few trees in Forsyth and Lumpkin county of northeast Georgia.  Pea to nickel-size hail also occurred with the stronger cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSB Television of Atlanta relayed a report of nickel-sized hail from the Dahlonega area.

The snow gradually increased in intensity through the remainder of the afternoon and into the early evening.  Numerous traffic accidents occurred during the evening rush. Snow, heavy at times, continued through the evening, tapering off by midnight. Total accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across northern Florida and then northeastward off the Georgia and South Carolina coast. Precipitation initially fell in the form of rain, but quickly changed over to snow in the late afternoon and evening hours as winds shifted to the north and allowed colder air to wrap back into the region. Heavy snow accumulated across much of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from locations surrounding Evans county Georgia indicate that between 2.0 and 3.0 inches of snow accumulated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across the deep south, spreading light snow over the mountains of northeast Georgia during the late afternoon. The snow mixed with sleet during the evening, before changing to mostly sleet after midnight, then changing to rain as temperatures warmed during the pre-dawn hours. Total accumulations of sleet and snow ranged from trace amounts, up to an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another deep upper low was moving across the southeastern U.S. bringing yet another widespread precipitation event to the southeast U.S.  However, in contrast to the system that brought widespread snow to the state just three days earlier, the surface air mass was on average 10-degrees warmer across the state. Thus, this event evolved mostly into a rain event for north and central Georgia.  Some snow was reported in the far north.  However, Dade county in extreme northwest Georgia was the only county to report any measurable snowfall, where 1.5 to 2.0 inches of snow fell on Sand Mountain.  Otherwise, snowfall measurements across the remaining north Georgia counties were a trace or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dade County 911 Center reported 1.5 to 2.0 inches of snow on Lookout Mountain and 1.5 inches of snow on Sand Mountain in the northern part of the county.  Elsewhere in the county, snowfall amounts were less than 1.0 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave embedded in a fast zonal flow was traversing the southern states.  A comparatively warm air mass, with respect to many previous winter events, was in place across the region as the associated cold front swept into the southeast states.  A strong line of thunderstorms accompanied the front and moved into the Georgia during the pre-dawn hours.  The thunderstorms brought considerable lightning, brief heavy rain, and some small hail.  None of the thunderstorms reached severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny-sized hail in the Thomaston area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Department personnel reported a tree down from non-thunderstorm winds on Hilltonia Road in Millen, Georgia.  Wind gusts were estimated to be between 40 and 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was present over the Southeast. A weak lee-side trough developed over the Midlands during the afternoon and spawned showers and thunderstorms along residual convective outflows from the previous day's thunderstorm activity. Thunderstorms then shifted over portions of southeast Georgia during the early evening hours. An inland moving sea breeze prolonged shower and thunderstorms through the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported numerous roads still not passable due to flooding near the intersections of Victory and Bull Street, Victory and Martin Luther King Street, Edgelawn and Pennsylvania Street, Tennessee and Pennsylvania Street and Gwinnett and Pennsylvania Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching the region in combination with the sea breeze in a moist and unstable atmosphere, resulted in scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down near the intersection of Old Louisville Road and Dean Forest Road, one mile west of Garden City, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a weak lee-side trough over the eastern midlands.  The thunderstorms developed and organized into a line as they met up with the inland pushing sea breeze across South Carolina.  Additional thunderstorms developed late across southeast Georgia as outflow boundary collisions occurred in a moist and unstable atmosphere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported three trees down, one on Grady Drive, one on Route 121, and another on Edgar Lane, approximately 5 miles south-southwest of Millen, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure aloft was moving slowly east through the mid-south.  Deep tropical moisture remained across the southeast U.S.  A weak cold front was approaching from the northwest.  Afternoon heating resulted in the development of scattered thunderstorms across much of north and central Georgia.  Many of the storms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  Some of the more significant issues this day were lightning related, with one lightning-related injury in Cobb county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coweta County 911 Center reported that a couple of trees and power lines were down approximately three miles southeast of Newnan on East Front Street and at the Oak Inn Hotel on U.S. Highway 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon along an inland surface trough west and northwest of the forecast area. These thunderstorms pushed outflow into environment featuring surface based CAPE values of 3500-5000 j/kg over the forecast area. As this occurred new multicell thunderstorms developed and exhibited pulse severe characteristics, especially where boundary interactions occurred. Several thunderstorms produced scattered reports of wind damage mainly along and west of interstate 95 from mid afternoon into the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down on State Route 17 North.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although a broad upper ridge continued across most of the southern states, a weakness in the upper ridge was noted across Georgia.  Weak, unstable northwest flow was also noted across Georgia, while a warm, moist, unstable air mass continued at the surface.  Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across east central Georgia producing damaging downburst winds and nickel to quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emanuel County 911 Center reported that several trees were down in the vicinity of Canoochee-Garfield and Johnson-Hall Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level ridge axis weakened as it shifted south during the early afternoon hours. Strong surface heating resulted in CAPE values around 5000 J/kg over the region. As the seabreeze began to shift inland a few strong thunderstorms developed. A nearly stationary frontal boundary became the focus of severe thunderstorms over the Midlands during the mid afternoon.  As this frontal boundary shifted southward a line of severe thunderstorms swept through portions of southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported extensive damage to a storage building roof along with numerous trees uprooted along Forhand Lane and Hilltonia Creek Road, 1 mile northeast of Hilltonia, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough of low pressure across the Central Savannah River Area in combination with the inland pushing sea breeze, resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 4 inch diameter tree limbs were reported down along Highway 57, five miles northwest of Townsend, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper low was moving from the mid-south into the southeast United States, while a deep upper trough and associated cold front was sweeping through the Ohio Valley into the northeast United States.  Deep tropical moisture remained across the southeast in advance of these systems.  Scattered thunderstorms developed across west central Georgia during the early afternoon and spread into the central and eastern parts of middle Georgia throughout the afternoon.  The main severe weather element on this day was damaging downburst winds.  Wind gusts of 63 and 82 mph were measured from Dodge and Houston counties, respectively, during severe thunderstorms.  One injury was reported from Laurens county from a falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public measured a wind gust of 63 mph in far northwest Dodge county near the Pulaski county line.  No damage was reported from the area, however.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad high pressure aloft continued across the southeastern states and was beginning to strengthen from previous days.  However, a very weak disturbance was evident across the north side of the high across north Georgia.  This was sufficient given strong heating to produce isolated to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  A few of these became strong and produced pea-sized hail and non-severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twiggs County 911 Center and Twiggs County Emergency Management Director reported that four trees were down across the north part of the county.  Trees were down on Sgoda Road, Fountain Road, Long Street Road, and Georgia Highway 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak short wave in the mountains shifted southeast and transitioned into a shear |axis that extended from the Midlands of South Carolina into southwest Georgia during the evening. Strong surface heating led to moderate instability along an inland moving sea breeze, which eventually sparked a round of scattered diurnal convection by mid afternoon. High PWAT and DCAPE values supported thunderstorms capable of producing heavy rain and damaging straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sylvania police department reported 1 tree and powerlines down on North Main Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down at the intersection of West 47th Street and Stevens Street in Savannah, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad and strong subtropical upper ridge was in place the mid-south into Florida.  The center of the ridge across Alabama and the Florida Panhandle provided Georgia with an unstable northwest flow aloft.  A weak backdoor front was evident across northeast Georgia with surface high pressure nosing southwest into Georgia.  Isolated storms developed on the east and southeast side of Atlanta during the mid-afternoon and propagated southwest onto the south side of Atlanta during the late afternoon before dissipating.  Nickel to quarter-sized hail, downburst winds, and frequent cloud-to-ground lightning were noted with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The DeKalb County 911 Center reported a lightning strike to a home on Radcliffe Boulevard.  No specific details were provided regarding details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and moist atmosphere set up during the early afternoon hours across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia, with CAPE values reaching between 2500-3000 j/kg over much of the forecast area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported dime to quarter size hail, 2 miles south-southwest of Sylvania, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms moved over of Georgia into during the late afternoon and interacted with a number of outflow boundaries and the sea breeze to initiate stronger thunderstorms. The atmosphere across the region was highly unstable with surface based CAPE between 3500 to 4000 J/kg and DCAPES near 1200 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter estimated wind gusts between 50 to 60 miles per hour.

Nickel to quarter-sized hail, downburst winds, and frequent cloud-to-ground lightning were noted with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported damage to two sides of a privacy fence and one tree down on Kingsbrook Road near Decatur.  The DeKalb County 911 Center reported that one power line was down on Newcastle Road in Lithonia.

These thunderstorms pushed outflow into environment featuring surface based CAPE values of 3500-5000 j/kg over the forecast area. As this occurred new multicell thunderstorms developed and exhibited pulse severe characteristics, especially where boundary interactions occurred. Several thunderstorms produced scattered reports of wind damage mainly along and west of interstate 95 from mid afternoon into the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down on Boggy Pond Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported that four residents of an apartment complex felt lightning go right through their building as it struck on Monday afternoon, July 12th, 2010.  The Pooler, Georgia Fire Department said the residents were inside a building at the Carlisle, formerly the Alta Town Lakes apartments, when the lightning hit their units at 385 Godley Station Boulevard.  They said no one was seriously injured.  There was no damage to the apartments.  One or the individuals said he was just standing by the refrigerator, opened it up and the next thing he knew, he felt something go straight through his hand.  It felt like it went down my spine and hit me in the head like somebody punched me in the face and then I felt it go out my foot, but it happened in just like a second, he said.  Next thing i know, I was on the ground, and it felt like the room was spinning, like fast, said resident John McDonald.  He said he had a massive headache, but was not seriously hurt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper low was moving from the mid-south into the southeast United States, while a deep upper trough and associated cold front was sweeping through the Ohio Valley into the northeast United States.  Deep tropical moisture remained across the southeast in advance of these systems.  Scattered thunderstorms developed across west central Georgia during the early afternoon and spread into the central and eastern parts of middle Georgia throughout the afternoon.  The main severe weather element on this day was damaging downburst winds.  Wind gusts of 63 and 82 mph were measured from Dodge and Houston counties, respectively, during severe thunderstorms.  One injury was reported from Laurens county from a falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Talbotton New Era reported that a thunderstorm downburst blew down the brick front wall of the remains of an old building in downtown Talbotton at the intersection of Washington Avenue and Monroe Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad high pressure aloft continued across the southeastern states and was beginning to strengthen from previous days.  However, a very weak disturbance was evident across the north side of the high across north Georgia.  This was sufficient given strong heating to produce isolated to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  A few of these became strong and produced pea-sized hail and non-severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck a home on Sydney Crest Lane.  The resulting fire caused moderate damage to an exterior wall and the attic.  The entire home suffered extensive smoke damage.  The fire appeared to start when the lightning struck the incoming electrical line to the home.  The family of four was displaced as a result of the incident.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail and tree limbs were blown down about 2.5 miles east-southeast of the Highway 341 and Highway 99 intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface ridge was across north Florida while a trough was draped across south central Georgia. A mid level short wave trough traversed southeast Georgia during the afternoon over a very moist and unstable air mass. Steering motion was slow but primarily to the east. Numerous storms fired across the area during the afternoon and evening as the sea breezes and their outflows converged between Highway 301 and Interstate 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported at the corner of Kight Street and Wiregrass Street in Homerville. Half inch size hail was also observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A emergency manager reported powerlines down in 8 locations around Darien, Georgia. A few homes had minor roof damage due to falling limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was present over the Southeast. A weak lee-side trough developed over the Midlands during the afternoon and spawned showers and thunderstorms along residual convective outflows from the previous day's thunderstorm activity. Thunderstorms then shifted over portions of southeast Georgia during the early evening hours. An inland moving sea breeze prolonged shower and thunderstorms through the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 2 trees down at a residence. The trees were 40 feet tall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in the mid levels traverse the area in the afternoon over an unstable and moist low level air mass. This energy combined with convergence along the sea breezes and resultant outflow boundaries created scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was north of the area with a high pressure ridge axis south of the region. Storms fired along the sea breezes and resultant outflows over a moist and unstable air mass with light steering flow in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired NWS employee reported 2 large pine trees snapped off about 10 feet above the ground near the Harrietts Bluff exit of Interstate 95. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper trough that had lingered across the northeast and mid-Atlantic region was moving out and a large vertically stacked high pressure area was rapidly replacing the trough across the northeast and mid-Atlantic.  During the next few days, this high pressure area would drift south toward Georgia, finally shutting off the incessant thunderstorm activity.  However, deep tropical moisture lingered along and south of a stationary front, that during the early morning was draped west-to-east across north central and northeast Georgia.  The front served as a focusing mechanism for persistent redevelopment of thunderstorms.  Two rounds of thunderstorms, several severe, plagued the area on August 27th.  The first occurred across north central and northeast Georgia, near Atlanta and Athens, during the early morning hours between midnight and 4 am EDT.  The second round occurred during the mid to late afternoon mainly across east central.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that five trees were blown down by a downburst from a small thunderstorm.  One of the trees fell on a home, just west of Rome, causing damage to the structure.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The La Grange Daily News reported that a large tree limb was blown from a tree during strong thunderstorm wind gusts causing it fall on a vehicle traveling on Broad Street near Springdale Street in La Grange.  Inside the vehicle were a mother and her three children.  Although none of the three children were injured, the mother had to be pulled from the vehicle and taken to a local hospital for surgery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front an upper-level trough were approaching the area from the northwest.  While the air mass across north and central Georgia had been relatively dry and stable for several days, low-level moisture was on the increase in advance of the front.  In addition, the remnants of Tropical Storm Hermine had become caught in the upper-level trough and front and contributed to an increase in thunderstorm activity across the region on this day.  A few of the storms became strong to briefly severe in north Georgia during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail near Smyrna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall during the pre-dawn across the mountains of northeast Georgia. After sunrise, snow became moderate to heavy at times, resulting in accumulations of 1 to 4 inches across most of the area by late morning. Snow, heavy at times, continued into the afternoon across the mountains, with heavy accumulations realized in most areas by early afternoon. By early evening, total snowfall ranged from 4 to 8 inches across the area, with localized amounts as high as 10 inches, especially in the higher elevations.

A cold front continued to approach Georgia from the northwest as a deep upper trough was rotating through the northeast U.S.  Unstable northwest flow aloft combined with the front and a very hot, humid air mass across Georgia to help develop scattered to numerous thunderstorms across the north part of the state during the afternoon and evening.  Only a couple of these thunderstorms became severe, but all produced frequent to intense cloud-to-ground lightning and very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Douglas County Emergency Management Director reported that four lightning-related fires occurred across the county between 549 pm EDT and 601 pm EDT as strong thunderstorms tracked from northwest to southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weak cold front that had entered north Georgia on the 6th had pushed south into central Georgia.  Northwest flow aloft continued around a deep upper low anchored in the northeast U.S.  However, the most unstable air had been pushed further south, mostly into south Georgia.  Just before sunset, outflow from thunderstorms in south Georgia pushed north into the southern portions of central Georgia, causing isolated thunderstorms to develop.  One of these briefly became severe in Wilcox county producing damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilcox County 911 Center reported that at least a dozen trees were down around Owensboro near Georgia Highway 233.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge persisted across Texas, but was beginning to build wastward into the mid-south and southeast U.S.  An upper trough was located across the northeast into the mid-Atlantic region.  This provided Georgia with unstable northwest flow aloft.  Meanwhile...a weak cold front was moving slowly south through central Georgia.  Hot temperatures and dewpoints in the low and mid 70s made for a very unstable atmosphere across the region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the mid-afternoon and persisted into the evening.  The most persistent and intense thunderstorms originated from a cluster of thunderstorms that developed in northeast Georgia and slowly worked its way south-southwest into central Georgia.  Intense lightning and damaging downburst winds were noted with some of the thunderstorms, along with very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barrow County 911 Center reported that five trees were down around Winder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow, mixed with rain at times, developed over the piedmont and foothills of northeast Georgia around sunrise. Despite bursts of moderate to heavy snow, a warm ground and above freezing temperatures caused much of the snow to melt upon impact. As a result, accumulations were light, ranging from trace amounts across southern piedmont areas, to an inch or so along the I-85 corridor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County Emergency Management Director reported that a few trees were blown down in the west central and central part of the county between Tails Creek and Ellijay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was over southeast Georgia. Storms initiated along the surface front as diurnal destabilization occurred, then resultant outflows fired additional convection along the sea breezes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was observed in Baxley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was over southeast Georgia. Storms initiated along the surface front as diurnal destabilization occurred, then resultant outflows fired additional convection along the sea breezes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds blew down trees along the West Green Highway between Douglas and West Green.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was over southeast Georgia. Storms initiated along the surface front as diurnal destabilization occurred, then resultant outflows fired additional convection along the sea breezes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered power lines were blown down across the west side of Baxley. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was north of the area with a high pressure ridge axis south of the region. Storms fired along the sea breezes and resultant outflows over a moist and unstable air mass with light steering flow in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to marble size hail was reported a mile east of Folkston. Gusty winds of 45 to 50 mph were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was north of the area with a high pressure ridge axis south of the region. Storms fired along the sea breezes and resultant outflows over a moist and unstable air mass with light steering flow in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was covering all lanes of Highway 1 in downtown Folkston causing major traffic slowdowns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was north of the area with a high pressure ridge axis south of the region. Storms fired along the sea breezes and resultant outflows over a moist and unstable air mass with light steering flow in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pine tree was blown down across Highway 40. Hail was observed, but the size unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was north of the area with a high pressure ridge axis south of the region. Storms fired along the sea breezes and resultant outflows over a moist and unstable air mass with light steering flow in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of trees blown down were received by the Charlton County Police Department. One tree fell on a home at 87 Shady Lane in Folkston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was north of the area with a high pressure ridge axis south of the region. Storms fired along the sea breezes and resultant outflows over a moist and unstable air mass with light steering flow in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired NWS employee reported large tree limbs and small pine trees blown down near Laurel Island Parkway. Winds were estimated between 60 and 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge persisted across Texas, but was beginning to build wastward into the mid-south and southeast U.S.  An upper trough was located across the northeast into the mid-Atlantic region.  This provided Georgia with unstable northwest flow aloft.  Meanwhile...a weak cold front was moving slowly south through central Georgia.  Hot temperatures and dewpoints in the low and mid 70s made for a very unstable atmosphere across the region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the mid-afternoon and persisted into the evening.  The most persistent and intense thunderstorms originated from a cluster of thunderstorms that developed in northeast Georgia and slowly worked its way south-southwest into central Georgia.  Intense lightning and damaging downburst winds were noted with some of the thunderstorms, along with very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny-sized hail around Fairplay on East Carroll Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong cold front for early August was moving through the eastern U.S., including the southeast U.S., supported by a vigorous upper trough in the northeast U.S.  With a very moist, unstable air mass remaining in place at the surface across the southeast U.S., scattered strong and isolated severe thunderstorms developed across mainly north Georgia during the afternoon.  Most of the damage was the result of thunderstorm downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County 911 Center reported that at least six trees and a few power lines were down northwest of Cartersville, mostly in the Sugar Valley Road area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong cold front for early August was moving through the eastern U.S., including the southeast U.S., supported by a vigorous upper trough in the northeast U.S.  With a very moist, unstable air mass remaining in place at the surface across the southeast U.S., scattered strong and isolated severe thunderstorms developed across mainly north Georgia during the afternoon.  Most of the damage was the result of thunderstorm downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County 911 Center reported that seven homes across the county were struck by lightning.  Damages ranged from minor to moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary approached the Altamaha River basin during the mid afternoon from the north. A quasi-stationary front was draped northwest to southeast near the basin. In addition, the low level atmosphere was unstable with CAPEs over 2500 J/kg and lapse rates were decreasing aloft as an upper level short wave trough approached from the northwest. A few isolated severe storms developed near the Altamaha River basin and drifted southeast while producing large hail and wind damage. Storm total rainfall amounts neared 4 inches across portions of Jeff Davis and Appling counties. These storms interacted with the east coast sea breeze over Brantley, Pierce and Ware counties where cells began to rotate...and continued to produce large hail and damaging winds. Additional storms developed across Clinch and Echols counties in the evening where the east and west coast sea breezes combined with outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Mershon along Highway 32.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over the gulf coast region with a southeast low level flow. Increased instability in the afternoon and enhanced upper level energy from the upper level low phased over southeast Georgia. Atmospheric ingredients favored wet downbursts in collapsing cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Douglas Enterprise reported a giant pecan tree blown down onto a home along New Hope Church Road in Ambrose. There was roof damage and damage to the eaves of the home. There was a significant weather alert in effect. The cost of damage was estimated. Around 6 inches of rainfall was estimated in the Ambrose area within a 48 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering long period swell originating from distant Hurricane Earl well east of the Georgia coast, resulted in numerous rip currents that prompted a significant number of rescues at the beaches in Tybee Island, Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tybee Island life guards reported that between 20 and 30 people were rescued from rip currents between 430 pm and 700 pm at the beaches of Tybee Island, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable atmosphere ahead of an approaching cold front, supported the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.  Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in regions where multiple boundary collisions occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel size hail and gusty winds, 1 mile south of Daisy, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable atmosphere ahead of an approaching cold front, supported the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.  Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in regions where multiple boundary collisions occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Evans county Emergency Manager conducted a damage survey and found an old barn was blown over and several 3 to 4 inch diameter tree limbs from pecan trees were knocked down.  All the damage was positioned lying in the same direction, and as a result it was determined that the damage was due to straight line winds from a thunderstorm downburst.  The damage observed was consistent with wind speeds near 65 mph.  Several trees were also reported down in sporadic locations within 1 mile of this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over extreme northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours. A couple of the storms became pulse severe, producing small areas of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple trees were blown down in the Carnesville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed over the region ahead of a cold front. A few of the storms produced damaging winds over extreme northeast Georgia during the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large limb and power lines were blown down on the south side of the city. Trees were also blow down on Brookdale Rd, and on highway 123.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large long period swells generated by Hurricane Earl as it passed by well to the east of the South Carolina and Georgia coasts, resulted in beach erosion at a couple of beach communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tybee Island lifeguards observed 8 to 10 foot breakers in the surf zone at the beaches on Tybee Island Georgia.  They estimated that about 20 feet of beach had been eroded by the high surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October 25th began a 3-day long period of active convective weather for north and central Georgia.  The culprit was a developing intense upper-level low and associated surface low located across the northern plains, which moved slowly east toward the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley region by the 27th.  Record low surface pressures were observed with this system in Wisconsin on the 26th.  A strong cold front developed from the surface cyclone southward into the Arklatex region by the 26th. Deep southerly flow in advance of the system brought unseasonably warm, moist air well northward across the eastern  U.S.  Even though the parent system was well north of Georgia, strong dynamics, a strong low-level jet, and the warm, unstable air mass provided a near perfect environment for scattered to numerous thunderstorms.  The slow movement of the system resulted in three consecutive days of strong to severe thunderstorms across north and central Georgia.||On the 25th, a leading vigorous short wave was tracking across the southern states.  This short wave resulted in severe thunderstorms and several tornadoes from east Texas eastward to Georgia.  Two tornadoes were confirmed with this event in northwest Georgia on the 25th, including an EF1 in southern Dade county.  These tornadoes were part of a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) or line of severe thunderstorms that moved across north Georgia during the early morning hours.  The line dissipated by late morning across east and central Georgia.  However, individual strong to severe thunderstorms redeveloped once again during afternoon heating across east central and southeast Georgia producing large hail and damaging wind gusts.  No tornadoes were observed with the second round of activity, however. |Unusually for October, this event ended up being one of the most significant severe weather outbreaks for the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens County Emergency Management Director reported that a few trees and power lines were down in the East Dublin area, with a few more trees and power lines down east of East Dublin toward Brewton.  A home near Brewton suffered minor roof damage.  A barn in the same area also sustained moderate roof damage.  Downed trees were noted along U.S. Highway 319 and Ernest Road, east of East Dublin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators relayed reports of quarter-sized hail just northwest of Dublin.  Hail was observed on Old Macon Road and Georgia Highway 338 and near the W. H. Bud Baron Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep full-latitude upper trough was sweeping from the central into the eastern U.S.  The trough brought a quick transition to the eastern U.S. from mild fall temperatures to an extended period of well below normal temperatures.  As the trough swept through the southeastern U.S. on November 30th, it took on a significant negative tilt.  Although only marginal instability was noted because of extensive pre-frontal cloud cover and light to moderate rain showers, a strong 50-60kt low-level jet accompanied the trough as it rotated through Georgia during the afternoon of November 30th.  This was sufficient combined with minimal instability (CAPE) in place at the time to support the development of a fairly well marked, yet very narrow, quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) which spawned two tornadoes, one an EF2 causing extensive damage to homes in Gwinnett county, and another weaker tornado in Henry county.  In addition, the squall line or QLCS also produced numerous damaging wind events across north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast office in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that a second, weaker EF0 tornado touched down within the same quasi-linear convective system that spawned the EF2 tornado in Gwinnett county.  This tornado originated in extreme western Butts county approximately four miles southwest of Jenkinsburg, crossed very briefly into extreme eastern Henry county, before crossing back into extreme western Butts county and then lifting immediately thereafter.  The tornado literally was only 30 feet or so within the Henry county line.  The tornado had a total path length of 2.5 miles, mostly in Butts county.  The maximum path width was determined to be 50 yards with maximum wind gusts estimated at 80 mph.  Damage in Henry county was confined to a gas station/convenience store, which lost part of its awning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a stationary front over the mountainous terrain of northeast Georgia. The storms produced a little wind damage and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a 7000-square foot lodge on Tuts Mountain Rd, destroying the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed south of a stationary front. A couple of the storms produced large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in the Nuberg area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved out of the North Carolina mountains and strengthened over the mountains of northeast Georgia. The line produced a little wind damage before moving into South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Harrison Bridge Rd between 3 and 4 miles south of Carnesville and along Atkinson Bridge Rd in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely intense upper-level low and associated record deep surface cyclone continued to move slowly from the north central U.S. into the Ohio Valley.  A slow moving, yet strong cold front extended from the surface cyclone southward into the Arklatex region.  The intensity of the system helped induce deep southerly flow in advance across the southeast and eastern U.S. with unseasonably warm, moist air being transported well northward.  Even though the parent system was well north of Georgia, strong dynamics, a strong low-level jet, and the warm, unstable air mass provided a near perfect environment for scattered to numerous thunderstorms.  On this day, Georgia was located between the departing short wave that sparked numerous severe thunderstorms on the 25th and the parent system approaching from the west.  Thus, convective activity was slightly less numerous and intense, with only minimal severe weather reports on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White County Sheriff's Office reported golf ball-sized hail on the southeast side of Cleveland near Truett McConnell College.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep full-latitude upper trough was sweeping from the central into the eastern U.S.  The trough brought a quick transition to the eastern U.S. from mild fall temperatures to an extended period of well below normal temperatures.  As the trough swept through the southeastern U.S. on November 30th, it took on a significant negative tilt.  Although only marginal instability was noted because of extensive pre-frontal cloud cover and light to moderate rain showers, a strong 50-60kt low-level jet accompanied the trough as it rotated through Georgia during the afternoon of November 30th.  This was sufficient combined with minimal instability (CAPE) in place at the time to support the development of a fairly well marked, yet very narrow, quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) which spawned two tornadoes, one an EF2 causing extensive damage to homes in Gwinnett county, and another weaker tornado in Henry county.  In addition, the squall line or QLCS also produced numerous damaging wind events across north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that an EF2 tornado touched down near Holland Park Drive in east central Gwinnett county, or about five miles northwest of Dacula.  The tornado then tracked slightly over two miles to the north-northeast between Mountain View High School and Twin Rivers Middle School before lifting just east of the intersection of Gravel Springs Road and Interstate-85.  The tornado was determined to have a path width of 300 yards with maximum wind gusts of 130 mph.  Extensive damage was observed to homes mainly in the Kirkstone Subdivision of Buford.  A total of 56 homes and one business along the path of the tornado sustained at least minor damage.  Of these, 15 to 20 homes sustained major damage or were destroyed and thus were declared uninhabitable.  One of these homes collapsed on itself.  Damage to homes and property was estimated by the state insurance commissioner to exceed $5 million.

Within Butts county four homes were damaged and at least 200 trees were blown down or uprooted along the path of the tornado.  Several outbuildings were damaged or destroyed, including six storage buildings blown across U.S. Highway 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike directly hit the Pierce County Emergency Operations Center. Major damage was done, and preliminary estimates were around $100,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning directly hit the Pierce County Emergency Operations Tower. The strike traveled down the tower and severely damage the center's control box for the tower, its server, radio for both the county EMS and Pierce County jail as well as radios for dispatch stations and telephone lines. The strike also destroyed lightning arrestors which were designed to alleviate impacts from lightning stirkes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of pecan trees were blown down near the intersection of Post Road and Chicora Road. One tree fell on a house and the other on a car. The cost of damage was estimated, and there were no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from sea breeze induced storms directly hit the steeple of the Pleasant Hill Baptist Church in Patterson, Ga during the evening. The church suffered significant damage to its electronics including its audio visual equipment and air conditioning system. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the steeple of the Pleasant Hill Baptist Church in Patterson, Ga. The time of the event was based on radar. The strike split the fiberglass steeple in half, and major damage was done to the church's electrical systems including the sound system, video equipment, projectors and air conditioning. Damage was estimated in the $10's of thousands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level short wave trough helped destabilize an unstable airmass and triggered a few severe storms during the afternoon along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down into power lines. The fire department was dispatched because residents detected smoke, but a fire did not develop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was over the area with a southwesterly low level flow over the area. Scattered precipitation developed along the sea breezes and outflow boundaries, and with slow storm motion and high precipitation content in the area storms produced periods of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rainfall, water pooling on the roof and structural failure caused the roof of the Family Dollar Store on 4420 Altama Avenue in the Brunswick area to collapse. One person was hospitalized due to injuries and 3 others suffered minor injuries. Radar indicated rainfall rates near 2 inches per hour, and the time of the event was estimated. The cost of damage was estimated at $20K and the cost of supply damage was estimated at $10K.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface ridge axis was across south Florida with broad troughing over south Georgia. A short wave trough was moving east over the Gulf Coast region with a swath of drier air over south Georgia. In addition, temperatures aloft decreased which assisted in destabilization. A strong storm produced a funnel cloud that was observed near Pearson in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atkinson County 911 Dispatch relayed a public report of a funnel cloud in Pearson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours. The front triggered thunderstorms over the southern mountains of North Carolina which then moved southeast, crossing much of northeast Georgia. Some of the storms produced areas of damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Rainbow Dr in Lavonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed across the area due to the sea breezes and an unstable airmass. Lightning hit one home near the Laura Walker State park in Hoboken and completely destroyed the 100 year old residence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Waycross Journal/Herald reported that lightning struck a 100 year old farmhouse about a mile from Laura Walker State Park during the afternoon. There were no injuries. The time of the fire was approximated based on radar imagery, and the lightning fire persisted into the early morning hours of the following day as fire fighters contained the blaze to just the house that it destroyed. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong backdoor cold front for the end of July was moving slowly southward through east and central Georgia.  Unstable northwest flow was present aloft around the eastern periphery of a strong subtropical ridge centered over north Texas.  A very hot, humid air mass remained in place across much of the state, but especially central Georgia south of the front.  This along with the added upper-level support provided ample support for scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorm development.  However, the most significant thunderstorm activity occurred during the late afternoon and evening when a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northeast Georgia between Gwinnett and Clarke counties and moved southeast toward Hancock and Jefferson counties before diminishing.  This thunderstorm complex produced damaging downburst winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dodge County Emergency Management Director reported that two trees and one power line were down near Eastman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was northwest of the area while Tropical Depression #3 was nearing south Florida. Between the two features a swath of dry air was positioned over north Florida and southeast Georgia. During the afternoon, a mid level lobe of positive vorticity moved over southeast Georgia from the northeast around the upper ridge. This sparked scattered showers and storms near the Altamaha River basin that tracked southward during the afternoon on the fringe of the dry air swatch. One storm became severe over Atkinson county. The cell produced a large outflow boundary that propagated south across Clinch, Lanier and Echols county's while weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light pole was blown down in Willacoochee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge was centered west of the local area over the northern Gulf of Mexico. Precipitable water was 2.25 inches off of the 12Z JAX sounding. Storm initiated along the sea breezes and outflow boundaries during the afternoon when instability was very high with surface based CAPES of 3000-5000 J/kg. Scattered severe storms developed during the late afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High heat indices of 110 or higher effected the Albany area during the afternoon of August 1st.
Severe thunderstorms rumbled across the CSRA producing more wet microburst that took down trees and powerlines.

Strong subtropical high pressure continued to dominate the southeastern United States.  Nonetheless, a very moist, hot, tropical-like air mass continued across the region.  With high temperatures in the 95 to 100 degree range, scattered diurnal pulse thunderstorms were the rule despite the high pressure aloft.  A few of these became strong and produced strong wind gusts as well as frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  However, none meeting severe criteria were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported that a tree was down across a road and a power lines near McCollum Road and Georgia Highway 54, near Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical ridge that had been over the area for several days, was beginning to break down and shift east and south of the area.  This was allowing a cold front to approach from the north.  In addition, an subtropical low was evident across Texas and this was slowing drifting northeast.  A hot, unstable, and very moist tropical atmosphere remained across the region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed quickly during the afternoon across north Georgia, generally from Rome, to Canton, to Gainesville and northward.  The added synoptic support in comparison to previous days resulted in an upswing in the intensity and coverage of storms across this area.  Many of the storms became strong and a few became severe producing damaging downburst winds and large hail.  All of the storms produced frequent, intense cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County 911 Center reported that a couple of trees were down.  At least one power line was down with power outages noted in Summerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across the deep south while the mid and upper level low moved over the Mississippi River Valley.  A surface low developed over south Georgia in the afternoon, and a trailing squall line moved across the area in the afternoon and evening. Strong low level shear, including a 50 to 60 knot low level jet, combined with adequate instability to produce widespread severe storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down onto power lines near Highway 252. The event time was based on radar.

Precipitation mainly fell as freezing rain, although some areas saw a period of light accumulating snow at the onset. By late morning, most areas had received at least a trace of ice accretion, resulting in very slippery roads.
Scattered severe thunderstorms produced some wind damage and flooding in Richmond county around Augusta.

Veterans in Chattooga County - 11/11/10:1500 LT to 11/11/10:1800 LT burning 6.5 acres.|(b).  Lake in Fannin County - 11/13/10:1415 LT to 11/13/10:1530 LT burning 1/4 acres.

The first fire took four days to contain.||The three wildfires were:|(a).  Mill Creek in Walker county from 10/16/10:2200 LT to 10/20/10:2000 LT burning 36.5 acres.|(b).  Rock Wall in Lumpkin county from 10/21/10:1830 LT to 10/21/10:2100 LT burning 1/4 acre.|(c).  Hall Road in Murray county from 10/22/10:1530 LT to 10/22/10:1530 LT burning 82.75 acres.||Estimated damages from these fires were in excess of $10,000.

Heavy rains fell in and around the Savannah metro near high tide, thus enhancing flooding to prone locations.

However, the most significant thunderstorm activity occurred during the late afternoon and evening when a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northeast Georgia between Gwinnett and Clarke counties and moved southeast toward Hancock and Jefferson counties before diminishing.  This thunderstorm complex produced damaging downburst winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sumter County Emergency Management Director reported that three trees and one power line were down between Croxton Crossroads and the west side of Americus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms in the western CSRA took down some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported a couple of trees down on Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms in the western CSRA took down some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported a few trees down on Hepzibah Church road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October 25th began a 3-day long period of active convective weather for north and central Georgia.  The culprit was a developing intense upper-level low and associated surface low located across the northern plains, which moved slowly east toward the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley region by the 27th.  Record low surface pressures were observed with this system in Wisconsin on the 26th.  A strong cold front developed from the surface cyclone southward into the Arklatex region by the 26th. Deep southerly flow in advance of the system brought unseasonably warm, moist air well northward across the eastern  U.S.  Even though the parent system was well north of Georgia, strong dynamics, a strong low-level jet, and the warm, unstable air mass provided a near perfect environment for scattered to numerous thunderstorms.  The slow movement of the system resulted in three consecutive days of strong to severe thunderstorms across north and central Georgia.||On the 25th, a leading vigorous short wave was tracking across the southern states.  This short wave resulted in severe thunderstorms and several tornadoes from east Texas eastward to Georgia.  Two tornadoes were confirmed with this event in northwest Georgia on the 25th, including an EF1 in southern Dade county.  These tornadoes were part of a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) or line of severe thunderstorms that moved across north Georgia during the early morning hours.  The line dissipated by late morning across east and central Georgia.  However, individual strong to severe thunderstorms redeveloped once again during afternoon heating across east central and southeast Georgia producing large hail and damaging wind gusts.  No tornadoes were observed with the second round of activity, however. |Unusually for October, this event ended up being one of the most significant severe weather outbreaks for the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia, confirmed that an EF1 tornado, which began in DeKalb county Alabama, crossed into Dade county approximately two miles southwest of Rising Fawn.  The tornado then tracked east-northeast, lifting approximately two miles east of Rising Fawn near the eastern most extension of Holder Loop Road.  The maximum path width was determined to be 150 yards with maximum wind gusts estimated at 100 mph.  Considerable damage to trees and property was noted along the path of the tornado.  Thirty homes sustained minor roof damage either from the strong wind or downed trees.  Two barns were destroyed and a mobile home lost nearly all of its roof.  Nearly 200 trees were uprooted, snapped, or blown over along the path of the tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fannin County 911 Center reported that more than half-a-dozen trees were down across the western part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The intense upper-level low and associated deep surface cyclone were moving further north into Ontario.  A cold front extended from the upper low southward into the lower Mississippi valley.  Unseasonably warm and moist air continued to stream north into the southeast U.S. in advance of this increasingly slow moving system.  However, upper dynamics were beginning to weaken in comparison with those in place earlier in the week.  Nonetheless, conditions still supported development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms.  The thunderstorms on the 27th, the third day of this large-scale synoptic system tended to be more single-cell and multi-cell in nature with less tendency toward QLCS development.  However, several of the thunderstorms on the 27th exhibited at least moderate rotation and prompted tornado warnings.  However, only one storm produced an EF0 tornado in Cherokee county during mid-afternoon, resulting in considerable damage to parts of the county just southeast of Holly Springs.  This was the final day of convective activity across the southeast U.S. associated with this large synoptic-scale system as cooler, drier air spread into the southeast U.S. behind the front for the remainder of the month courtesy of a large Canadian high pressure system building eastward from the central U.S.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oconee County Emergency Management Director reported damage to several trees and power lines in the far southeast part of Oconee County, approximately six to seven miles southeast of Watkinsville.  One vehicle suffered damage from a fallen tree.  The Emergency Management Director was suspicious that the damage appeared to be caused by a tornado as there was a path of damage and trees and power lines were twisted along the path of damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that at least a dozen trees were down on the east and southeast side of Eatonton.  Several power lines were also down.  Some roads were blocked.  Widespread power outages were noted in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely intense upper-level low and associated record deep surface cyclone continued to move slowly from the north central U.S. into the Ohio Valley.  A slow moving, yet strong cold front extended from the surface cyclone southward into the Arklatex region.  The intensity of the system helped induce deep southerly flow in advance across the southeast and eastern U.S. with unseasonably warm, moist air being transported well northward.  Even though the parent system was well north of Georgia, strong dynamics, a strong low-level jet, and the warm, unstable air mass provided a near perfect environment for scattered to numerous thunderstorms.  On this day, Georgia was located between the departing short wave that sparked numerous severe thunderstorms on the 25th and the parent system approaching from the west.  Thus, convective activity was slightly less numerous and intense, with only minimal severe weather reports on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hall County Sheriff's Office reported that one tree and one power line were down between White Sulfer Road and Georgia Highway 365, approximately two miles northeast of Gainesville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the Cherokee County Emergency Management Director confirmed that a weak EF0 tornado had touched down approximately four miles east-southeast of Holly Springs.  The tornado first touched down on Oak Meadow Drive in a well populated residential neighborhood, then tracked east-northeast approximately 3/4 mile before lifting near the intersection of Waters Road and East Cherokee Drive.  The maximum path width was determined to be 75 yards wide with maximum wind gusts estimated to be 75 mph.  Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted along the path of the tornado.  A number of trees fell on homes resulting in major damage to three homes and minor damage to another 10 homes.  Three outbuildings and three vehicles were destroyed by the fallen trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast office in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that a second, weaker EF0 tornado touched down within the same quasi-linear convective system that spawned the EF2 tornado in Gwinnett county.  This tornado originated in extreme western Butts county approximately four miles southwest of Jenkinsburg, crossed very briefly into extreme eastern Henry county, before crossing back into extreme western Butts county and then lifting immediately thereafter.  The tornado had a total path length of 2.5 miles, mostly in Butts county.  The maximum path width was determined to be 50 yards with maximum wind gusts estimated at 80 mph.  This final portion of the tornado lasted less than one minute and covered a distance of only 0.1 miles within Butts county.  Damage was confined to a few trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked eastward from the Gulf of Mexico with a surface warm front extending toward the southern Florida peninsula. Cold air was in place across the area as well as deep moisture. As an upper level short wave trough approached the area from the west, reports of sleet began across portions of southeast Georgia during the late morning hours. By evening, widespread reports of snowfall were reported with total amounts from 1 to 2 inches in some areas by 2 am on Feb. 13, 2010.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow become the dominant precipitation type around 6:17 pm per the emergency manager in Pearson and Willacoochee. There was minor accumulation noted on vehicles. By 11:30 pm that night, the county central dispatch reported about 2 inches of snow had accumulated across the county with some icy roadways.

The first three weeks of October brought mostly dry weather conditions to north Georgia preceded by an unusually hot and dry September.  As a result, conditions were favorable for wildfires.  Three human-caused wildfires were observed in October between the 16th and 22nd.  The first fire took four days to contain.||The three wildfires were:|(a).  Mill Creek in Walker county from 10/16/10:2200 LT to 10/20/10:2000 LT burning 36.5 acres.|(b).  Rock Wall in Lumpkin county from 10/21/10:1830 LT to 10/21/10:2100 LT burning 1/4 acre.|(c).  Hall Road in Murray county from 10/22/10:1530 LT to 10/22/10:1530 LT burning 82.75 acres.||Estimated damages from these fires were in excess of $10,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry commission reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Mill Creek burned 36.5 acres and took four days to contain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions (D2) developed across the remainder of southwest and south-central Georgia and continued into December.
This is a continuation of the wildfire events that began on October 16th.  This episode incorporates that third wildfire event of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Hall Road burned 46 acres and caused $7,000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep eastern U.S. trough penetrating well south, even toward Cuba, allowed a bitter cold Arctic air mass to surge into the southeast U.S.  This brought some of the coldest weather to north and central Georgia that had been observed in years.  Atlanta recorded its coldest high temperature and its first high temperature below 30 degrees since February 1996.  On December 13th, the high temperature in Atlanta only reached 29 degrees Fahrenheit.  Low temperatures fell into the teens across much of north Georgia for several mornings, with single digit readings in the mountains.  Strong northwest winds supporting significant low-level cold advection contributed to significant orographic lifting across the north Georgia mountains.  Moisture and upper dynamics were limited, but sufficient when combined with the strong cold advection to bring snow showers and average snowfall of about one inch across many of the north Georgia mountain counties, generally north of Summerville, to Jasper, to Cleveland line, with a dusting of snow toward the north side of the Atlanta metropolitan area.  Some of the northwest facing slopes of the mountains received two to four inches of snow, with one report of five inches in parts of northeast Gilmer county.  With surface temperatures remaining well below freezing for an extended period of time, the snow easily accumulated on the ground.||The following is a summary of the maximum snowfall totals for north Georgia counties during this event, which began around noon on December 12th and ended around noon on December 13th.||Bartow county - a dusting;|Catoosa county - 1/2 inch;|Chattooga county - 1/2 inch or less;|Cobb county - a dusting;|Dade county - 1.0 inch;|Dawson county - 1/2 inch in lower to 2.0 inches in higher elevations;|Fannin county - 1.0 inch in lower to 2.0 inches in higher elevations;|Floyd county - a dusting;|Forsyth county - a dusting;|Gilmer county - 2.0 inches in lower to 5.0 inches at elevations above 2000 feet in the northeast part of the county;|Gordon county - a dusting;|Hall county - a dusting;|Lumpkin county - a dusting;|Murray county - a dusting to less than 1/2 inch;|Pickens county - generally 1.0 inch lower to 1.5 inches higher elevations;|Towns county - 2.0 inches, except isolated 3.0 inch totals at the higher elevations;|Union county - generally 1.0 inch of snow throughout through the county|Walker county - 1.0 inch of snow, with up to 2.0 inches in the higher elevations;|White county - 1/2 inch lower elevations to 1.0 inch in the higher elevations;|Whitfield county - dusting to less than 1/2 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Union County 911 Center reported that snowfall averaged around one inch across most of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant Arctic air mass, which had spread across the southeast U.S. beginning on the 12th, was retreating slowly northeast.  By mid-afternoon on the 15th, mid-level warm advection, combined with a weak disturbance aloft, resulted in the development of light precipitation across north and central Georgia.  Much of north Georgia, north of Interstate-20, remained just below freezing as the precipitation spread into the area from the west during the mid-afternoon.  Ground surfaces were at or below freezing as the result of the previous three days of all-day below freezing temperatures and early morning temperatures in the 10-15F range.  In addition, surface dewpoints at the onset of the precipitation event were near 0F across north Georgia, resulting in a significant wetbulb effect.  Surface temperatures dropped quickly at the onset of the precipitation from the mid 30s into the upper 20s.  Thus, although liquid equivalent precipitation averaged only 0.01 inch across most of the affected area, it froze immediately on contact with the surface.  An initial burst of sleet quickly changed over to light freezing rain and eventually freezing drizzle through the evening hours as the boundary layer air mass had undergone significant warming and was well above freezing.  Roads, including major interstates from Atlanta west to Alabama and north to Tennessee, quickly and literally became like ice skating rinks as a thin sheet of black ice (<0.1 inch) formed on the roads and highways.  To make matters worse, this occurred during the 5 pm to 7 pm evening rush hour.  Major highways and interstates north and west of Atlanta, such as Interstate-20 and Interstate-75, became completely clogged with accidents.  Thousands of motorists became stranded in multiple car pileups and backups.  In some cases, a quick trip became several hours long.  Over 3000 accidents occurred across north Georgia during this evening, with at least 1000 of these occurring just in the Atlanta metropolitan counties alone.  While no fatalities were reported, several injuries occurred. By mid-afternoon on the 15th, mid-level warm advection, combined with a weak disturbance aloft, resulted in the development of light precipitation across north and central Georgia.  Much of north Georgia, north of Interstate-20, remained just below freezing as the precipitation spread into the area from the west during the mid-afternoon.  Ground surfaces were at or below freezing as the result of the previous three days of all-day below freezing temperatures and early morning temperatures in the 10-15F range.  In addition, surface dewpoints at the onset of the precipitation event were near 0F across north Georgia, resulting in a significant wetbulb effect.  Surface temperatures dropped quickly at the onset of the precipitation from the mid 30s into the upper 20s.  Thus, although liquid equivalent precipitation averaged only 0.01 inch across most of the affected area, it froze immediately on contact with the surface.  An initial burst of sleet quickly changed over to light freezing rain and eventually freezing drizzle through the evening hours as the boundary layer air mass had undergone significant warming and was well above freezing.  Roads, including major interstates from Atlanta west to Alabama and north to Tennessee, quickly and literally became like ice skating rinks as a thin sheet of black ice (<0.1 inch) formed on the roads and highways.  To make matters worse, this occurred during the 5 pm to 7 pm evening rush hour.  Major highways and interstates north and west of Atlanta, such as Interstate-20 and Interstate-75, became completely clogged with accidents.  Thousands of motorists became stranded in multiple car pileups and backups.  In some cases, a quick trip became several hours long.  Over 3000 accidents occurred across north Georgia during this evening, with at least 1000 of these occurring just in the Atlanta metropolitan counties alone.  While no fatalities were reported, several injuries occurred. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep eastern U.S. trough penetrating well south, even toward Cuba, allowed a bitter cold Arctic air mass to surge into the southeast U.S.  This brought some of the coldest weather to north and central Georgia that had been observed in years.  Atlanta recorded its coldest high temperature and its first high temperature below 30 degrees since February 1996.  On December 13th, the high temperature in Atlanta only reached 29 degrees Fahrenheit.  Low temperatures fell into the teens across much of north Georgia for several mornings, with single digit readings in the mountains.  Strong northwest winds supporting significant low-level cold advection contributed to significant orographic lifting across the north Georgia mountains.  Moisture and upper dynamics were limited, but sufficient when combined with the strong cold advection to bring snow showers and average snowfall of about one inch across many of the north Georgia mountain counties, generally north of Summerville, to Jasper, to Cleveland line, with a dusting of snow toward the north side of the Atlanta metropolitan area.  Some of the northwest facing slopes of the mountains received two to four inches of snow, with one report of five inches in parts of northeast Gilmer county.  With surface temperatures remaining well below freezing for an extended period of time, the snow easily accumulated on the ground.||The following is a summary of the maximum snowfall totals for north Georgia counties during this event, which began around noon on December 12th and ended around noon on December 13th.||Bartow county - a dusting;|Catoosa county - 1/2 inch;|Chattooga county - 1/2 inch or less;|Cobb county - a dusting;|Dade county - 1.0 inch;|Dawson county - 1/2 inch in lower to 2.0 inches in higher elevations;|Fannin county - 1.0 inch in lower to 2.0 inches in higher elevations;|Floyd county - a dusting;|Forsyth county - a dusting;|Gilmer county - 2.0 inches in lower to 5.0 inches at elevations above 2000 feet in the northeast part of the county;|Gordon county - a dusting;|Hall county - a dusting;|Lumpkin county - a dusting;|Murray county - a dusting to less than 1/2 inch;|Pickens county - generally 1.0 inch lower to 1.5 inches higher elevations;|Towns county - 2.0 inches, except isolated 3.0 inch totals at the higher elevations;|Union county - generally 1.0 inch of snow throughout through the county|Walker county - 1.0 inch of snow, with up to 2.0 inches in the higher elevations;|White county - 1/2 inch lower elevations to 1.0 inch in the higher elevations;|Whitfield county - dusting to less than 1/2 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker County 911 Center reported snowfall of one inch in the lower elevations to two inches in the higher elevations.
