EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Microbursts from isolated severe thunderstorms produced wind damage in the northern CSRA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees down at the intersection of Hwy 220 and Hwy 601.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system and associated cold front brought heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total rainfall of 6.05 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front had stalled across south Georgia.  An upper-level trough was exiting to the east of the area as a broad upper ridge began to build northward from the Gulf.  Isolated thunderstorms developed during the heat of the afternoon across central Georgia near the front.  One storm in Dodge county briefly became severe producing quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dodge County Emergency Management Director reported quarter-sized hail in the Rhine area.  Radar data strongly suggested that hail of similar size fell southward to the Telfair county line east of Browning.  One tree was also down along Georgia Highway 117 between Rhine and the Telfair county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed in association with the sea-breeze and a weak upper level trough.  One storm became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal framed greenhouse partially collapsed and an oak tree was uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was west northwest of the area. A pre-frontal surface trough extended from the middle Atlantic State to the Gulf of Mexico Coast. A cold front was across extreme northern Georgia. Storm initiated in the afternoon and evening along the thermal trough in advance of the pre-frontal trough in an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on East Dame Avenue. Residents reported other trees down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was west northwest of the area. A pre-frontal surface trough extended from the middle Atlantic State to the Gulf of Mexico Coast. A cold front was across extreme northern Georgia. Storm initiated in the afternoon and evening along the thermal trough in advance of the pre-frontal trough in an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down across roadways southeast of Jesup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was west northwest of the area. A pre-frontal surface trough extended from the middle Atlantic State to the Gulf of Mexico Coast. A cold front was across extreme northern Georgia. Storm initiated in the afternoon and evening along the thermal trough in advance of the pre-frontal trough in an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near Lucille Drive. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a cold front approaching from the west and the sea breeze pushing inland from the Atlantic, resulted in scattered thunderstorms across portions of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 2 mobile homes shifted slightly off their foundations near the intersection of Motes Road and Old Portal Road.  Surrounding buildings also received dents from hail.  The damage occurred 5 miles north of Portal, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms moved over of Georgia into during the late afternoon and interacted with a number of outflow boundaries and the sea breeze to initiate stronger thunderstorms. The atmosphere across the region was highly unstable with surface based CAPE between 3500 to 4000 J/kg and DCAPES near 1200 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported trees down on powerlines on Cypress Lake Road near the Highway 301 Bypass. The road is currently closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cutoff upper low remained over Alabama, but was weakening further and starting to become absorbed in the mean flow.  By May 31st, the upper low was lifting northeast toward the mid-Atlantic and exerting less influence on the weather across the area.  Scattered showers and thunderstorms once again developed in southeast to northwest oriented bands during the afternoon and evening.  As with previous days, a few of these became strong to severe and many produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny to quarter-sized hail in the far northern part of the county northwest of Payne City, near Wesleyan.  In addition, the public estimated wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.  Several trees limbs were blown down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed upper low was moving southeast from the middle Tennessee Valley into the southeast United States.  An associated frontal system and low pressure area was moving from the eastern Gulf coastal region northeastward along the southeastern U.S. seaboard.  An unusually warm, moist atmosphere was present across the southeast U.S. in advance of this weather system.  Although overall instability was marginal, strong shear was present with this weather system.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia throughout the day.  While most of the storms were non-severe, one storm in northwest Georgia produced a significant downburst.  The storm simultaneously produced large hail, which when combined with the downburst winds produced a significant path of damage across Whitfield county.  Another storm during the evening spawned a brief EF0 tornado in the central Georgia county of Dooly, with very minimal damage noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny to nickel-sized hail across the north part of the county, especially in the Klondike Road area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent Arctic air mass remained in place across the eastern half of the United States.  A new mass of Arctic air was rapidly spreading south and east from the central Plains behind a cold front located moving southeast from the Arkalatex region.  A deep upper low was digging southeastward from the Midwest.  Although moisture was limited because of the cold Arctic air mass already in place ahead of the front, upper support provided ample support for a brief period of light snow and some light sleet to spread across the north Georgia area during the afternoon and evening hours of January 7th.  Because temperatures had been at or below freezing much of the previous seven days and temperatures at the time of the onset of the precipitation were near freezing, ice and snow quickly accumulated on roads and ground surfaces.  Accumulations of snow were mostly in the one inch range northwest of Interstate-85, with some two to three inch accumulations in the north Georgia mountains.  Some sleet was mixed in at the onset of the precipitation.  Areas southeast of Interstate-85 saw mostly 1/2 inch accumulations of snow, with just a trace of snow or sleet in central Georgia counties.  With a fresh Arctic air mass surging into the area January 8th - 9th, the ice and snow remained on the ground for several days.  The cold antecedent conditions resulted in an extended period of ice and snow covered roads in many north Georgia counties, despite the overall rather light accumulations of winter precipitation.||The following is a list of average snowfall reports received from north and central Georgia counties between 4 pm EST on January 7th and midnight EST on January 7th.||Banks county received 0.5 inch.|Barrow county received 0.5 inch. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Arctic cold front moved through the southeast early on the 10th as a deep upper low developed along the mid-Atlantic coast.  A corresponding deep surface low was intensifying along the mid-Atlantic seaboard.  This synoptic pattern produced a tight pressure gradient behind the front across north Georgia.  Wind gusts of 45 to 50 mph (which meets local WFO FFC high wind warning criteria) were observed at the Brasstown Bald RAWS station along the Union/Towns county line from midnight until 5 am in the morning.  Wind gusts elsewhere were mostly in the 20 to 30 mph range across north Georgia.  A few trees were blown down in White and Lumpkin county.  A couple of power lines were impacted as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White County 911 Center reported that four trees were blown down near Unicoi State Park and Georgia Highway 356.  A couple of power lines were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow aloft, resulting from a deep upper trough off the east coast, brought a cold, dry air mass into Georgia.  Strong, gusty northwest winds combined with the dry air mass to fuel a human-caused wildfire in Whitfield county of northwest Georgia.  The fire burned 81 acres, took seven hours to contain, and resulted in nearly $14,000 in damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported that a human-caused wildfire in Whitfield county, labeled the Redwine Cove fire, burned 81 acres, took seven hours to contain, and resulted in $13,671 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent Arctic air mass remained in place across the eastern half of the United States.  A new mass of Arctic air was rapidly spreading south and east from the central Plains behind a cold front located moving southeast from the Arkalatex region.  A deep upper low was digging southeastward from the Midwest.  Although moisture was limited because of the cold Arctic air mass already in place ahead of the front, upper support provided ample support for a brief period of light snow and some light sleet to spread across the north Georgia area during the afternoon and evening hours of January 7th.  Because temperatures had been at or below freezing much of the previous seven days and temperatures at the time of the onset of the precipitation were near freezing, ice and snow quickly accumulated on roads and ground surfaces.  Accumulations of snow were mostly in the one inch range northwest of Interstate-85, with some two to three inch accumulations in the north Georgia mountains.  Some sleet was mixed in at the onset of the precipitation.  Areas southeast of Interstate-85 saw mostly 1/2 inch accumulations of snow, with just a trace of snow or sleet in central Georgia counties.  With a fresh Arctic air mass surging into the area January 8th - 9th, the ice and snow remained on the ground for several days.  The cold antecedent conditions resulted in an extended period of ice and snow covered roads in many north Georgia counties, despite the overall rather light accumulations of winter precipitation.||The following is a list of average snowfall reports received from north and central Georgia counties between 4 pm EST on January 7th and midnight EST on January 7th.||Banks county received 0.5 inch.|Barrow county received 0.5 inch. Areas southeast of Interstate-85 saw mostly 1/2 inch accumulations of snow, with just a trace of snow or sleet in central Georgia counties.  With a fresh Arctic air mass surging into the area January 8th - 9th, the ice and snow remained on the ground for several days. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, negatively tilted upper low was rotating northeast from the southern plains and mid-south region of the U.S.  An associated low pressure system was located along the Alabama and Florida Gulf Coast, moving eastward, with a warm front located across south Georgia and south Alabama.  Gulf and Pacific moisture were riding over the warm front.  Widespread precipitation fell across north and central Georgia from late on the 4th through the 5th.  Rainfall amounts of two to four inches were common.  A split flow pattern aloft was keeping a cold air mass in place across the eastern U.S., which had prevailed much of the 2009-2010 winter season.  While the air mass at this particular time was not abnormally cold for early February, it was sufficiently cold at the onset of the precipitation, especially in the higher elevations of the northeast Georgia mountains, that some freezing rain, sleet, and snow fell for several hours.  Accumulations of winter precipitation were generally confined to elevations at or above 2500 feet.||Strong winds were also observed with this intense, large-scale synoptic system.  The most significant wind occurred shortly after 1200 am on the 5th when a band of heavy rain showers moved from the southwest to the northeast across north Georgia.  Bartow county fared the worst where around 200 trees were blown down in the Rydal area.  Pickens county just to the east saw at least 25 downed trees, including one which fell on a house.||Many rivers also went into flood stage across north and central Georgia.||The following are some of the winter weather reports reported to the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia.||Dawson county reported a light accumulation of sleet in the Amicalola Falls area, with no significant problems.  This occurred in the higher elevations of the northwest part of the county.||Lumpkin county reported some sleet, but no accumulation.||Towns county reported less than 1/8 inch of ice and snow, but enough to result in numerous accidents across the county.||Union county reported up to two inches of snow at the higher elevations, at or above 3500 feet, near Suches.  Portions of Georgia Highway 180 were closed.||White county reported up to 1/4 inch of ice and snow, most at the higher elevations in the north part of the county.  Portions of Georgia Highways 17, 75, and 366 were closed in the north part of the county, generally north of Helen.  Several accidents occurred and some motorists were stranded in their vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White County Emergency Management Director reported from 1/4 to 1/8 inch of sleet, ice, and snow across the northern part of the county.  Numerous accidents were reported and several motorists were stranded in their vehicles.  Georgia Highways 17, 75, and 366, mostly north of Helen, were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, negatively tilted upper low was rotating northeast from the southern plains and mid-south region of the U.S.  An associated low pressure system was located along the Alabama and Florida Gulf Coast, moving eastward, with a warm front located across south Georgia and south Alabama.  Gulf and Pacific moisture were riding over the warm front.  Widespread precipitation fell across north and central Georgia from late on the 4th through the 5th.  Rainfall amounts of two to four inches were common.  A split flow pattern aloft was keeping a cold air mass in place across the eastern U.S., which had prevailed much of the 2009-2010 winter season.  While the air mass at this particular time was not abnormally cold for early February, it was sufficiently cold at the onset of the precipitation, especially in the higher elevations of the northeast Georgia mountains, that some freezing rain, sleet, and snow fell for several hours.  Accumulations of winter precipitation were generally confined to elevations at or above 2500 feet.||Strong winds were also observed with this intense, large-scale synoptic system.  The most significant wind occurred shortly after 1200 am on the 5th when a band of heavy rain showers moved from the southwest to the northeast across north Georgia.  Bartow county fared the worst where around 200 trees were blown down in the Rydal area.  Pickens county just to the east saw at least 25 downed trees, including one which fell on a house.||Many rivers also went into flood stage across north and central Georgia.||The following are some of the winter weather reports reported to the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia.||Dawson county reported a light accumulation of sleet in the Amicalola Falls area, with no significant problems.  This occurred in the higher elevations of the northwest part of the county.||Lumpkin county reported some sleet, but no accumulation.||Towns county reported less than 1/8 inch of ice and snow, but enough to result in numerous accidents across the county.||Union county reported up to two inches of snow at the higher elevations, at or above 3500 feet, near Suches.  Portions of Georgia Highway 180 were closed.||White county reported up to 1/4 inch of ice and snow, most at the higher elevations in the north part of the county.  Portions of Georgia Highways 17, 75, and 366 were closed in the north part of the county, generally north of Helen.  Several accidents occurred and some motorists were stranded in their vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pickens County 911 Center reported that strong winds, associated with a band of heavy rain showers, blew down at least 25 trees across the county.  One tree fell on a home on Grandview Road, but no significant damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent Arctic air mass remained in place across the eastern half of the United States.  A new mass of Arctic air was rapidly spreading south and east from the central Plains behind a cold front located moving southeast from the Arkalatex region.  A deep upper low was digging southeastward from the Midwest.  Although moisture was limited because of the cold Arctic air mass already in place ahead of the front, upper support provided ample support for a brief period of light snow and some light sleet to spread across the north Georgia area during the afternoon and evening hours of January 7th.  Because temperatures had been at or below freezing much of the previous seven days and temperatures at the time of the onset of the precipitation were near freezing, ice and snow quickly accumulated on roads and ground surfaces.  Accumulations of snow were mostly in the one inch range northwest of Interstate-85, with some two to three inch accumulations in the north Georgia mountains.  Some sleet was mixed in at the onset of the precipitation.  Areas southeast of Interstate-85 saw mostly 1/2 inch accumulations of snow, with just a trace of snow or sleet in central Georgia counties.  With a fresh Arctic air mass surging into the area January 8th - 9th, the ice and snow remained on the ground for several days.  The cold antecedent conditions resulted in an extended period of ice and snow covered roads in many north Georgia counties, despite the overall rather light accumulations of winter precipitation.||The following is a list of average snowfall reports received from north and central Georgia counties between 4 pm EST on January 7th and midnight EST on January 7th.||Banks county received 0.5 inch.|Barrow county received 0.5 inch. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent Arctic air mass remained in place across the eastern half of the United States.  A new mass of Arctic air was rapidly spreading south and east from the central Plains behind a cold front located moving southeast from the Arkalatex region.  A deep upper low was digging southeastward from the Midwest.  Although moisture was limited because of the cold Arctic air mass already in place ahead of the front, upper support provided ample support for a brief period of light snow and some light sleet to spread across the north Georgia area during the afternoon and evening hours of January 7th.  Because temperatures had been at or below freezing much of the previous seven days and temperatures at the time of the onset of the precipitation were near freezing, ice and snow quickly accumulated on roads and ground surfaces.  Accumulations of snow were mostly in the one inch range northwest of Interstate-85, with some two to three inch accumulations in the north Georgia mountains.  Some sleet was mixed in at the onset of the precipitation.  Areas southeast of Interstate-85 saw mostly 1/2 inch accumulations of snow, with just a trace of snow or sleet in central Georgia counties.  With a fresh Arctic air mass surging into the area January 8th - 9th, the ice and snow remained on the ground for several days.  The cold antecedent conditions resulted in an extended period of ice and snow covered roads in many north Georgia counties, despite the overall rather light accumulations of winter precipitation.||The following is a list of average snowfall reports received from north and central Georgia counties between 4 pm EST on January 7th and midnight EST on January 7th.||Banks county received 0.5 inch.|Barrow county received 0.5 inch. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent Arctic air mass remained in place across the eastern half of the United States.  A new mass of Arctic air was rapidly spreading south and east from the central Plains behind a cold front located moving southeast from the Arkalatex region.  A deep upper low was digging southeastward from the Midwest.  Although moisture was limited because of the cold Arctic air mass already in place ahead of the front, upper support provided ample support for a brief period of light snow and some light sleet to spread across the north Georgia area during the afternoon and evening hours of January 7th.  Because temperatures had been at or below freezing much of the previous seven days and temperatures at the time of the onset of the precipitation were near freezing, ice and snow quickly accumulated on roads and ground surfaces.  Accumulations of snow were mostly in the one inch range northwest of Interstate-85, with some two to three inch accumulations in the north Georgia mountains.  Some sleet was mixed in at the onset of the precipitation.  Areas southeast of Interstate-85 saw mostly 1/2 inch accumulations of snow, with just a trace of snow or sleet in central Georgia counties.  With a fresh Arctic air mass surging into the area January 8th - 9th, the ice and snow remained on the ground for several days.  The cold antecedent conditions resulted in an extended period of ice and snow covered roads in many north Georgia counties, despite the overall rather light accumulations of winter precipitation.||The following is a list of average snowfall reports received from north and central Georgia counties between 4 pm EST on January 7th and midnight EST on January 7th.||Banks county received 0.5 inch.|Barrow county received 0.5 inch. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed upper low was moving southeast from the middle Tennessee Valley into the southeast United States.  An associated frontal system and low pressure area was moving from the eastern Gulf coastal region northeastward along the southeastern U.S. seaboard.  An unusually warm, moist atmosphere was present across the southeast U.S. in advance of this weather system.  Although overall instability was marginal, strong shear was present with this weather system.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia throughout the day.  While most of the storms were non-severe, one storm in northwest Georgia produced a significant downburst.  The storm simultaneously produced large hail, which when combined with the downburst winds produced a significant path of damage across Whitfield county.  Another storm during the evening spawned a brief EF0 tornado in the central Georgia county of Dooly, with very minimal damage noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained storm spotter observed penny-sized hail near Ellerslie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep Arctic air mass persisted across north Georgia during the first two weeks of January.  Gusty northwest winds and a dry air mass were common during this period. The dry air mass and strong winds combined to fuel a human-caused wildfire in Lumpkin county.  The fire affected an area of 20 acres, burned for five hours, and resulted in $4500 in damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported that a human-caused wildfire fueled by strong northwest winds and low relative humidity burned 20 acres, took five hours to contain, and resulted in $4500 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed upper low was moving southeast from the middle Tennessee Valley into the southeast United States.  An associated frontal system and low pressure area was moving from the eastern Gulf coastal region northeastward along the southeastern U.S. seaboard.  An unusually warm, moist atmosphere was present across the southeast U.S. in advance of this weather system.  Although overall instability was marginal, strong shear was present with this weather system.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia throughout the day.  While most of the storms were non-severe, one storm in northwest Georgia produced a significant downburst.  The storm simultaneously produced large hail, which when combined with the downburst winds produced a significant path of damage across Whitfield county.  Another storm during the evening spawned a brief EF0 tornado in the central Georgia county of Dooly, with very minimal damage noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County Emergency Management Director reported that rainfall of 2.0 to 2.5 inches over a 2-hour period had resulted in six to seven inches of water on some roads, particularly in the Bonaire area.  Street flooding was observed at the intersection of Georgia Highway 96 and Georgia Highway 247.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper type system was moving quickly through the east and southeast U.S. bringing a surge of unusually cold air into the region.  Moisture was quite limited in the wake of the strong storm system that had just passed through the area on the 25th.  However, even with the lack of surface moisture and instability, very strong dynamics and cold air aloft allowed showers and even a few thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening as the cold front and upper trough swept through the region.  The activity was characterized by very little lightning, but a very low freezing level and strong evaporative cooling allowed wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph and dime to penny-sized hail to accompany many of the showers and thunderstorms that passed through the area during the afternoon and evening hours.  Temperatures dropped nearly 20 degrees from the 70s into the 50s with the passage of the showers as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail in downtown Atlanta and on the east side of Atlanta near Little Five Points.  Most of the hail was pea size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad surface and surface trough were positioned across south Georgia. The trough extended east of the surface low toward the Atlantic Coast. A prevailing southwest flow regime was in place over a very unstable atmosphere. Afternoon high temperatures rose into the mid to upper 90s with dew points in the lower 80s. Convection first initiated along both the east and west coast sea breeze fronts. A strong outflow boundary raced southward from convection that was over Duval county, and this feature triggered numerous strong to severe storms within a multicell line across north Florida. Across Southeast Georgia, storms rotated north of the quasi-stationary surface front that gradually shifted southward through the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive tree damage occurred in Nicholls between 530 pm and 6 pm local time as a gust front moved through from the northwest and a strong low level rotation traversed the city from the north. After the emergency manager conducted a survey, it was determined that the damage was downburst related. There were several reports of trees that were blown down on cars, and 7 homes suffered damage from trees. The cost of damage was estimated.

A home on Fifth Street near downtown sustained minor damage when a large tree fell on the structure.  The roof and guttering on the back of the home was damaged.  Power was out in the Newnan area for nearly an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weak, closed off upper low that had been lingering across the mid-south for the past few days was being absorbed into the mean flow and was lifting northeast across the region as an open trough.  A moist, unstable air mass continued across the region.  The added upper support provided and orographic lift across the terrain of northeast Georgia resulting in training of showers and thunderstorms across several northeast Georgia counties between Athens and Gainesville.  Rainfall of four to six inches fell across parts of Barrow and Jackson counties in less than 24 hours.||Note, this event continued into June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barrow County 911 Center reported flash flooding in parts of Barrow county as repeated showers and thunderstorms brought four to six inches of rain to the county.  The heaviest rainfall and flash flooding occurred in the eastern part of the county.  Rainfall of 5.9 inches was observed in a 24-hour period near the intersection of Pleasant Hill and Church Roads.  Flowing water reached a depth of one foot through several yards of homes along Breckenridge Trail, Pleasant Hill, and Church Roads approximately four miles east of Winder.  Three-hour and six-hour flash flood guidance ratios on the NWS FFMP program reached 150% of ratio in these areas.  Street flooding was also observed on Phillips Drive and Giles Road.  Monetary damage in the form of erosion, damaged culvert pipes, and debris removal was estimated to be $20,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jackson County Emergency Management Director reported flash flooding in the Arcade area in the southwest part of the county.  A small creek was flowing over Hunter Road in Arcade.  Culvert pipes were washed out along portions of McCreery and Hunter Roads.  Rainfall of four to five inches was common in southwest Jackson county over a 24-hour period.  Monetary damage in the form of erosion and damaged culvert pipes was estimated to be $10,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure aloft was moving slowly east through the mid-south.  Deep tropical moisture remained across the southeast U.S.  A weak cold front was approaching from the northwest.  Afternoon heating resulted in the development of scattered thunderstorms across much of north and central Georgia.  Many of the storms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  Some of the more significant issues this day were lightning related, with one lightning-related injury in Cobb county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fayette County Emergency Management Director reported that six lightning strike incidents occurred during evening thunderstorms.  No damage was reported with two of the lightning strikes.  On Brogdon Road, north of Fayetteville, a storage shed was struck causing $2500 in damages.  On Deer Cove, just north of Fayetteville, lightning struck a home and resulted in $5000 in damages.  In downtown Fayetteville on Church Street, a church was struck resulting in $30,000 in damages to the structure.  Finally, on Shamrock Drive, just south of Fayetteville, a home suffered $5000 in damages from a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weak, closed off upper low that had been lingering across the mid-south during late May was becoming completely absorbed into the westerlies across the mid-Atlantic region.  A moist, unstable air mass remained across the region.  Heavy showers and thunderstorms continued from the afternoon of May 31st into June 1st.  Additional showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across north and central Georgia.  Rainfall of four to six inches fell across parts of Barrow and Jackson counties in less than 24 hours, with one to two inches of rain common across many northeast Georgia counties.  Flash flooding in Barrow and Jackson counties continued into the early morning hours of June 1st.

Monetary damage in the form of erosion and damaged culvert pipes was estimated to be $10,000.||This flash flooding is a continuation of flash flooding that began during the evening of May 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure aloft was moving slowly east through the mid-south.  Deep tropical moisture remained across the southeast U.S.  A weak cold front was approaching from the northwest.  Afternoon heating resulted in the development of scattered thunderstorms across much of north and central Georgia.  Many of the storms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  Some of the more significant issues this day were lightning related, with one lightning-related injury in Cobb county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSB Television of Atlanta reported that lightning struck and injured a man hiking at the Kennesaw National Battlefield Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weak, closed off upper low that had been lingering across the mid-south during late May was becoming completely absorbed into the westerlies across the mid-Atlantic region.  A moist, unstable air mass remained across the region.  Heavy showers and thunderstorms continued from the afternoon of May 31st into June 1st.  Additional showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across north and central Georgia.  Rainfall of four to six inches fell across parts of Barrow and Jackson counties in less than 24 hours, with one to two inches of rain common across many northeast Georgia counties.  Flash flooding in Barrow and Jackson counties continued into the early morning hours of June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Times Herald of Newnan reported that lightning struck an outbuilding on Jack Russell Road just off Mount Carmel Road in the Welcome community of western Coweta county.  The structure and all of its contents were a total loss from the resulting fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although a broad upper ridge continued across most of the southern states, a weakness in the upper ridge was noted across Georgia.  Weak, unstable northwest flow was also noted across Georgia, while a warm, moist, unstable air mass continued at the surface.  Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across east central Georgia producing damaging downburst winds and nickel to quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jefferson County 911 Center reported that several trees and power lines were down in the northwest and west central part of the county, north through west of Louisville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pattern of recent days continued with a weakness across Georgia in an otherwise broad subtropical upper ridge that dominated the southern U.S.  Weak, unstable northwest flow aloft continued across Georgia, while a warm, moist, unstable air mass remained at the surface. Again, only isolated activity was noted, but a few of the storms once again became severe producing damaging downburst winds.  This time the severe thunderstorms were located in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reports that a few trees and large tree limbs were down along House Road in north Fulton county near Roswell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed and a couple of storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Worth county 911 center reported nine trees and two power lines down across the northern part of the county including along portions of state routes 300 and 313.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical ridge that had persisted across the southern U.S. was shifting further east off the southeast U.S. coast.  A deep upper trough was moving through the Ohio Valley.  The tail end of this trough was moving through the mid-south and into the southeast U.S.  With a hot, very moist air mass remaining in place scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon.  The most concentrated area of coverage was across west central into middle Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours when a mesoscale convective complex evolved and moved east-southeast across the area.  Widespread damaging wind gusts were observed as the complex tracked from near Columbus toward Vidalia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens County Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail near Interstate-16 near and east to southeast of Dexter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although high pressure aloft continued across the southern U.S., an upper trough was moving through the northeast U.S.  A weak trough extended from the main low pressure area in the northeast into the southeast U.S.  With very warm, moist, unstable air remaining in place across the region, scattered to numerous thunderstorms develop during the afternoon and evening once again.  Several of the storms became strong and produced non-severe wind gusts and hail.  Isolated storms reached severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post reported that lightning struck a home on Mitford Lane.  The home suffered extensive damage from fire and smoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed and a couple of storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 74 mph gust was measured at the Albany Southwest Georgia Regional Airport from a thunderstorm downburst.  This downburst also extended northeast of the airport to 1701 S Slappey Blvd where the Albany Herald Newspaper reported that a roof was peeled back and a steeple was toppled at the Harvest Kingdom Jubilee Ministries Church.  Several cars were also damaged from the debris.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper type system was moving quickly through the east and southeast U.S. bringing a surge of unusually cold air into the region.  Moisture was quite limited in the wake of the strong storm system that had just passed through the area on the 25th.  However, even with the lack of surface moisture and instability, very strong dynamics and cold air aloft allowed showers and even a few thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening as the cold front and upper trough swept through the region.  The activity was characterized by very little lightning, but a very low freezing level and strong evaporative cooling allowed wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph and dime to penny-sized hail to accompany many of the showers and thunderstorms that passed through the area during the afternoon and evening hours.  Temperatures dropped nearly 20 degrees from the 70s into the 50s with the passage of the showers as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail just east and southeast of Smyrna, especially near the intersection of Interstate-75 and Interstate-285.  Most of the hail was pea size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper trough, lifting out of the mid-south, combined with deep moisture and an unstable air mass to generate several rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  A number of these thunderstorms became severe during the afternoon and evening producing large hail and damaging winds.  Morning rain and thunderstorms stabilized the air mass across the northern part of the state.  Thus, the majority of the severe thunderstorm events were across central Georgia, especially between Macon and Vidalia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens County 911 Center reported that at least two dozen trees had been blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the evening hours as an inland moving sea breeze interacted with differential heating boundaries. A few multi-cell thunderstorms became strong with damaging winds and heavy rainfall as they entered an environment that had surface based CAPE values around 3000 j/kg and DCAPE values over 800 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and power lines down in Millen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inland trough and seabreeze initiated showers and thunderstorms in an unstable atmosphere during the afternoon and evening hours. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed during early overnight hours as a back door cold front lingered  in persistent unstable conditions. High DCAPES suggested thunderstorms capable of producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 1 tree down on Highway 144 between Highway 17 and Interstate 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough was shifting slowly east from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A leading cold front was located from New York to Alabama.  A summerlike subtropical ridge across the southeast U.S. was slowly shifting off the southeast U.S. coast.  The highly meridional flow had allowed an unusually deep tropical air mass to spread far northward across the eastern half of the U.S.  This weather system had been responsible for widespread catastrophic flooding in western and middle Tennessee during the previous three days.  As the system moved toward Georgia, it began to weaken considerably and the main dynamics lifted northeast more toward the mid-Atlantic.  Nonetheless, the slow movement of the system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to the area with two-day rainfall of 3-4 inches.  Flash flooding was observed in several counties on the northwest and west side of Atlanta, some of the same counties that experienced catastrophic flooding during late September 2009.  Flash flooding during this event was far less significant.  Severe weather was isolated and confined to east central Georgia toward the end of the event on the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was indicated along the USGS stream gage on Sweetwater Creek at the Interstate-20 bridge where it reached its flood stage of 10.0 feet at 745 am EDT.  The creek continued to rise and crested at 11.0 feet at 930 am EDT.  The Douglas County Emergency Management Director indicated that a small dam near Post and Liberty roads was breached.  A pond on Rose Avenue also overtopped its dam and flooded parts of Rose Avenue with six inches of water.  Several roads in the county were closed because of slow drainage.  Several homes suffered flooded basements as well.  Damages to roads and property were estimated at $50,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper trough, lifting out of the mid-south, combined with deep moisture and an unstable air mass to generate several rounds of showers and thunderstorms. Morning rain and thunderstorms stabilized the air mass across the northern part of the state.  Thus, the majority of the severe thunderstorm events were across central Georgia, especially between Macon and Vidalia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed half-dollar-sized hail in the far southeast corner of Dodge county between Jay Bird Springs and the Telfair county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An weak upper trough continued to move from the southern plains toward the mid-Atlantic region.  A very warm and unstable air mass remained in place across the southeast United States.  Afternoon temperatures in the middle to upper 90s and deep moisture created a particularly unstable atmosphere across Georgia.  Convective Available Potential Energy (CAPE) values rose to in excess of 3000 J/KG during the afternoon.  Consequently, scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed, many of which became strong to severe.  Damaging downburst winds were noted with many of the storms.  As the storms moved into east Georgia, several of the storms produced quarter to golf ball-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker County 911 Center reported that more than a dozen trees were down across the northern half of the county from near Cooper Heights to Rossville.  One of the trees fell on a home on Mountain View Circle causing damage to the structure.  Trees were down on Georgia Highway 341, Garretts Chapel Road, Crittenden Avenue, Camp Road, Pine Hurst Circle, Ireland Lane, Clebourne Avenue, Crescent Avenue, Simonton Drive, Trion Highway, and High Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical ridge across the southeast U.S. continued to weaken and shift east of Georgia.  A strong upper trough, for early July, and associated cold front were approaching from the northwest.  A very warm, moist air mass remained in place across the southeast states in advance of these systems.  This provided ample support once again for scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon.  On this particular day, activity was generally confined to north central and northeast Georgia, mostly north of Interstate-20 and east of Interstate-75.  With the exception of one large hail event in northeast Georgia, all of the severe events on this day were in the form of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County 911 Center reported that three to four trees were down in the north central part of the county, generally south to southeast of Adairsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous upper low had developed off the North Carolina/South Carolina coast in response to an upper trough which had previously been moving through the westerlies.  The southern part of the upper trough was left lagging behind along the southeast U.S. coast.  The upper trough then closed off as a weak upper low and meandered along the southeast U.S. coast for several days.  A weak surface low developed near the upper low.  Maritime air was brought back into Georgia with the northeast surface flow.  Northeast winds aloft also prevailed across Georgia.  Thunderstorms developed during the mid-afternoon in favorable areas of orographic lifting across the northeast Georgia mountains, then propagated southwest into north central and northwest Georgia.  During the early evening, a few of these became strong to severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.  The severe storm affected northwest Gordon and far northern Floyd counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gordon County 911 Center reported that at least 10 trees and several power lines were down in the far northwestern part of the county from just west of Sugar Valley to the Floyd county line.  Georgia Highway 136 was closed because of a downed tree and power line.  Two other roads were partially blocked from downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weak, closed off upper low that had been lingering across the mid-south during late May was becoming completely absorbed into the westerlies across the mid-Atlantic region.  A moist, unstable air mass remained across the region.  Heavy showers and thunderstorms continued from the afternoon of May 31st into June 1st.  Additional showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across north and central Georgia.  Rainfall of four to six inches fell across parts of Barrow and Jackson counties in less than 24 hours, with one to two inches of rain common across many northeast Georgia counties.  Flash flooding in Barrow and Jackson counties continued into the early morning hours of June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck the attic of a home on Goldfield Trace in the Forest Plantation subdivision.  The attic suffered significant damage from the resulting fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere was present as hot and humid conditions led to surface based CAPE values between 3000 to 3500 J/KG and low level lapse rates of  between 8 to 9 degrees Celsius. Inverted-v soundings and DCAPES between 1000 to 1200 J/KG suggested thunderstorms capable of producing strong downbursts as strong heating occurred during the afternoon and evening hours. Storms first began to initiate during the afternoon along an inland moving seabreeze. The focus then switched to inland areas where a Midland trough was approaching the southeast coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported half dollar size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough was shifting slowly east from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A leading cold front was located from New York to Alabama.  A summerlike subtropical ridge across the southeast U.S. was slowly shifting off the southeast U.S. coast.  The highly meridional flow had allowed an unusually deep tropical air mass to spread far northward across the eastern half of the U.S.  This weather system had been responsible for widespread catastrophic flooding in western and middle Tennessee during the previous three days.  As the system moved toward Georgia, it began to weaken considerably and the main dynamics lifted northeast more toward the mid-Atlantic.  Nonetheless, the slow movement of the system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to the area with two-day rainfall of 3-4 inches.  Flash flooding was observed in several counties on the northwest and west side of Atlanta, some of the same counties that experienced catastrophic flooding during late September 2009.  Flash flooding during this event was far less significant.  Severe weather was isolated and confined to east central Georgia toward the end of the event on the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Information and evidence received from the Baldwin County Emergency Management Agency and Fox 24 Television of Macon, Georgia, along with a subsequent tornado survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, GA, confirmed that an EF0 tornado tracked across northern Baldwin county, from just northwest to just northeast of Milledgeville.  The tornado touched down near the intersection of Pettigrew Road and Willie J. Brown Jr. Road, or approximately 3/4 mile west-southwest of the intersection of Georgia Highway 22 and Pettigrew Road.  From here, the tornado traveled on a sporadic approximately eight mile-long path to the east-northeast, just north of Milledgeville, ending at the western edge of the Milledgeville Country Club, along North Jefferson Street just north of Log Cabin Road. The maximum path width was estimated to be 75 yards wide and maximum wind gusts were estimated to be 75 to 80 mph.  The majority of the damage occurred at the beginning and ending points of the tornado, with just sporadic tree damage in between.  At the beginning point along Pettigrew Road, several large hardwood trees were either snapped or uprooted.  A portion of a metal roof was ripped off one side of a home and tossed into the neighboring trees.  At the ending point of the tornado, along North Jefferson Street, a porch was ripped off the side of one home and scattered about the adjacent woods.  The adjacent home suffered minor to moderate roof damage.  Several trees in the area were either uprooted or snapped.  Portions of North Jefferson Street were blocked for several hours from downed trees.  Several large hardwood trees were downed just west of the Milledgeville Country Club's maintenance facility, where the tornado appeared to lift.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical ridge across the southeast U.S. continued to weaken and shift east of Georgia.  A strong upper trough, for early July, and associated cold front were approaching from the northwest.  A very warm, moist air mass remained in place across the southeast states in advance of these systems.  This provided ample support once again for scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon.  On this particular day, activity was generally confined to north central and northeast Georgia, mostly north of Interstate-20 and east of Interstate-75.  With the exception of one large hail event in northeast Georgia, all of the severe events on this day were in the form of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed half-dollar-sized hail near Delhi, northeast of Tignall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An weak upper trough continued to move from the southern plains toward the mid-Atlantic region.  A very warm and unstable air mass remained in place across the southeast United States.  Afternoon temperatures in the middle to upper 90s and deep moisture created a particularly unstable atmosphere across Georgia.  Convective Available Potential Energy (CAPE) values rose to in excess of 3000 J/KG during the afternoon.  Consequently, scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed, many of which became strong to severe.  Damaging downburst winds were noted with many of the storms.  As the storms moved into east Georgia, several of the storms produced quarter to golf ball-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Off-duty National Weather Service employees observed five trees down between Peachtree City or the intersection of Georgia Highway 54 and Georgia Highway 74 and TDK Boulevard.  The public also observed one tree down near Starrs Mill in the southeast part of the county.

A mobile home on Laurenwood Road was destroyed by a downed tree and trees were down on two power lines on Macon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper trough, lifting out of the mid-south, combined with deep moisture and an unstable air mass to generate several rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  A number of these thunderstorms became severe during the afternoon and evening producing large hail and damaging winds.  Morning rain and thunderstorms stabilized the air mass across the northern part of the state.  Thus, the majority of the severe thunderstorm events were across central Georgia, especially between Macon and Vidalia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny-sized hail was observed at the Newnan Fire Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous upper low had developed off the North Carolina/South Carolina coast in response to an upper trough which had previously been moving through the westerlies.  The southern part of the upper trough was left lagging behind along the southeast U.S. coast.  The upper trough then closed off as a weak upper low and meandered along the southeast U.S. coast for several days.  A weak surface low developed near the upper low.  Maritime air was brought back into Georgia with the northeast surface flow.  Northeast winds aloft also prevailed across Georgia.  Thunderstorms developed during the mid-afternoon in favorable areas of orographic lifting across the northeast Georgia mountains, then propagated southwest into north central and northwest Georgia.  During the early evening, a few of these became strong to severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.  The severe storm affected northwest Gordon and far northern Floyd counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that three trees and two power lines were blown down in the Grantville area from a small isolated thunderstorm downburst.  Hail just less than dime-size was also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance continues to move northeast across the area on the northwest side of a subtropical ridge which had moved southward across north Florida.  A hot, humid, very unstable air mass remained in place across the region.  Ample tropical moisture was also in place at the surface.  This provided the support for yet another afternoon and evening of scattered thunderstorms.  While the thunderstorms were not as numerous or as widespread as the previous day, several of the storms that did develop became strong to severe.  One storm produced a significant downburst across Lumpkin county downing over 200 trees and causing damage to homes, businesses, and schools across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattahoochee County Emergency Management Director reported golf ball-sized hail just east of Cusseta.  The public observed nickel-sized hail in Cusseta.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carroll County Emergency Management Director reported that several road drainage culverts were washed out across the county.  Culverts were washed out on Hamp Chappell Road seven miles southeast of Carrollton, Horsley Mill Road near Little Snake Creek eight miles east of Carrollton, and at Dashboard Road 10 miles southwest of Carrollton.  At the height of the flooding, 18 roads across the county were closed.  Road repair cost the county $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough of low pressure aloft continued across the western Atlantic.  Deep north to north-northeast flow was noted aloft across the southeastern U.S.  A backdoor cold front was moving southwest into South Carolina and Georgia.  Considerable moisture and instability was present across the region as the front drifted southwest into northeast Georgia.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the region in advance of the front, then drifted southwest with the mean upper flow.  Several of the storms became severe producing quarter-sized hail and damaging downburst winds.  In addition, frequent to excessive cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with the storms.  At least one lightning-related fire was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported quarter-sized hail from the Chattahoochee National Forest area around Ramhurst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was present over the Southeast. A weak lee-side trough developed over the Midlands during the afternoon and spawned showers and thunderstorms along residual convective outflows from the previous day's thunderstorm activity. Thunderstorms then shifted over portions of southeast Georgia during the early evening hours. An inland moving sea breeze prolonged shower and thunderstorms through the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported approximately 25 trees uprooted and power lines down around the Glennville to Mendes areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County Emergency Management Director reported that at least a dozen trees were down across the northeast part of the county on the west side of Lake Lanier.  At least five trees were down in the Pilgrim Mill area, with one of these trees down on a home and another down on a vehicle.  Several of the downed trees also impacted power lines.  Trees were down on power lines on Holtzclaw Road, Tanglewood Drive, Shady Grove Road, and Jensen Trail and along Pilgrim Mill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front was moving southwest from the mid-Atlantic region into the southeast U.S.  A hot, humid, unstable air mass remained in place in advance of the front, which helped to support the development of scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon.  Several of the storms across north Georgia became severe during the afternoon and producing damaging wind gusts.  Isolated strong thunderstorms also occurred across central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gordon County 911 Center relayed a report of penny-sized hail along Interstate-75 southeast of Calhoun to near the Bartow county line.  Two trees were also blown down in this same area.

Portions of Six Flag were submerged in standing water during the height of the heavy rain and flooding.  No damage was reported at the park, however.  Damage across the county was confined to minor debris removal from areas adjacent to the creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that one large tree was blown down onto a home in the far southwest part of the county near Lake Allatoona and two large trees were down on power lines seven miles west of Woodstock near the Cobb county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although high pressure aloft continued across the southern U.S., an upper trough was moving through the northeast U.S.  A weak trough extended from the main low pressure area in the northeast into the southeast U.S.  With very warm, moist, unstable air remaining in place across the region, scattered to numerous thunderstorms develop during the afternoon and evening once again.  Several of the storms became strong and produced non-severe wind gusts and hail.  Isolated storms reached severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clarke County 911 Center reported that one tree and one power line were down near Athens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere was present as hot and humid conditions led to surface based CAPE values between 3000 to 3500 J/KG and low level lapse rates of  between 8 to 9 degrees Celsius. Inverted-v soundings and DCAPES between 1000 to 1200 J/KG suggested thunderstorms capable of producing strong downbursts as strong heating occurred during the afternoon and evening hours. Storms first began to initiate during the afternoon along an inland moving seabreeze. The focus then switched to inland areas where a Midland trough was approaching the southeast coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 6 to 7 trees down within a 1 mile stretch on Highway 25. The public also reported dime size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance continues to move northeast across the area on the northwest side of a subtropical ridge which had moved southward across north Florida.  A hot, humid, very unstable air mass remained in place across the region.  Ample tropical moisture was also in place at the surface.  This provided the support for yet another afternoon and evening of scattered thunderstorms.  While the thunderstorms were not as numerous or as widespread as the previous day, several of the storms that did develop became strong to severe.  One storm produced a significant downburst across Lumpkin county downing over 200 trees and causing damage to homes, businesses, and schools across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Meriwether County Emergency Management Director reported that three trees were down between Greenville and Woodbury.  Some power outages were noted in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough of low pressure aloft continued across the western Atlantic.  Deep north to north-northeast flow was noted aloft across the southeastern U.S.  A backdoor cold front was moving southwest into South Carolina and Georgia.  Considerable moisture and instability was present across the region as the front drifted southwest into northeast Georgia.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the region in advance of the front, then drifted southwest with the mean upper flow.  Several of the storms became severe producing quarter-sized hail and damaging downburst winds.  In addition, frequent to excessive cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with the storms.  At least one lightning-related fire was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County Emergency Management Director reported quarter-sized hail in the extreme western part of Gilmer county near the Murray county line, between Tails Creek and the Gilmer county line along Georgia Highway 282/U.S. Highway 76.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper trough, lifting out of the mid-south, combined with deep moisture and an unstable air mass to generate several rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  A number of these thunderstorms became severe during the afternoon and evening producing large hail and damaging winds.  Morning rain and thunderstorms stabilized the air mass across the northern part of the state.  Thus, the majority of the severe thunderstorm events were across central Georgia, especially between Macon and Vidalia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Peach County Emergency Management Director reported that three trees were down in the far east part of the county, east of Miami Valley.  Amateur radio operators estimated wind gusts of 50 mph or better in the Fort Valley area.  However, no specific damage reports were received from this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An weak upper trough continued to move from the southern plains toward the mid-Atlantic region.  A very warm and unstable air mass remained in place across the southeast United States.  Afternoon temperatures in the middle to upper 90s and deep moisture created a particularly unstable atmosphere across Georgia.  Convective Available Potential Energy (CAPE) values rose to in excess of 3000 J/KG during the afternoon.  Consequently, scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed, many of which became strong to severe.  Damaging downburst winds were noted with many of the storms.  As the storms moved into east Georgia, several of the storms produced quarter to golf ball-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County 911 Center reported that three trees were down between Ellijay and Mountaintown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough was shifting slowly east from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A leading cold front was located from New York to Alabama.  A summerlike subtropical ridge across the southeast U.S. was slowly shifting off the southeast U.S. coast.  The highly meridional flow had allowed an unusually deep tropical air mass to spread far northward across the eastern half of the U.S.  This weather system had been responsible for widespread catastrophic flooding in western and middle Tennessee during the previous three days.  As the system moved toward Georgia, it began to weaken considerably and the main dynamics lifted northeast more toward the mid-Atlantic.  Nonetheless, the slow movement of the system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to the area with two-day rainfall of 3-4 inches.  Flash flooding was observed in several counties on the northwest and west side of Atlanta, some of the same counties that experienced catastrophic flooding during late September 2009.  Flash flooding during this event was far less significant.  Severe weather was isolated and confined to east central Georgia toward the end of the event on the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several creeks across Fulton county reached or exceeded flood stage during this period.  This information was verified by USGS stream gages on the creeks.  Proctor Creek at Jackson Parkway in Atlanta reached its flood stage of 13.0 feet at 655 am EDT.  The creek continued to rise and crested at 14.4 feet at 815 am EDT.  Flood stage on Peachtree Creek at Northside Drive of 18.0 feet was reached at 815 am EDT.  The creek continued to rise and crested at 19.2 feet at 1215 pm EDT.  On Nancy Creek at Rickenbacker Drive, major flood stage of 13.0 feet was reached at 1105 am EDT.  The creek eventually crested at 13.2 feet at 1200 pm EDT before finally beginning to fall.  The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that at least 50 homes were affected by the flood waters of Nancy and Peachtree Creeks.  A swift water rescue was required along Nancy Creek where a female had to be removed from the top of a home on Fairfield Road approximately four miles west of North Atlanta.  Flood waters covered portions of Cochran Mill Road, Cascade-Palmetto Highway, and Vandiver Road at Amen Road in central Fulton county, all a result of local creeks out of their banks.  Portions of Interstate-20 west of Atlanta were closed during the height of the flooding.  Damages to property and roads from the flooding were estimated to be $500,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pattern of recent days continued with a weakness across Georgia in an otherwise broad subtropical upper ridge that dominated the southern U.S.  Weak, unstable northwest flow aloft continued across Georgia, while a warm, moist, unstable air mass remained at the surface.  A weak backdoor cold front lingered across the southern portion of the mid-Atlantic region.  Again, only isolated activity was noted, but a few of the storms once again became severe producing damaging downburst winds.  This time the severe thunderstorms were located in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed penny-sized hail in downtown Woodstock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weak, closed off upper low that had been lingering across the mid-south during late May was becoming completely absorbed into the westerlies across the mid-Atlantic region.  A moist, unstable air mass remained across the region.  Heavy showers and thunderstorms continued from the afternoon of May 31st into June 1st.  Additional showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across north and central Georgia.  Rainfall of four to six inches fell across parts of Barrow and Jackson counties in less than 24 hours, with one to two inches of rain common across many northeast Georgia counties.  Flash flooding in Barrow and Jackson counties continued into the early morning hours of June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jackson County Fire and Rescue Department reported that lightning struck a home on Traditions Way in Jefferson.  No other specific details were provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure aloft was moving slowly east through the mid-south.  Deep tropical moisture remained across the southeast U.S.  A weak cold front was approaching from the northwest.  Afternoon heating resulted in the development of scattered thunderstorms across much of north and central Georgia.  Many of the storms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  Some of the more significant issues this day were lightning related, with one lightning-related injury in Cobb county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cobb County 911 Center reported that four trees were down in the western part of the county approximately six to eight miles southwest to west-southwest of Marietta.  Two of the trees were down on power lines.  One tree each was down on Bullard Road, Bell Street, Midway Road at Dallas Highway.  Two trees were down on Villa Rica Road.  Power lines were down on Bullard Road at Villa Rica Road and on Bell Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although high pressure aloft continued across the southern U.S., an upper trough was moving through the northeast U.S.  A weak trough extended from the main low pressure area in the northeast into the southeast U.S.  With very warm, moist, unstable air remaining in place across the region, scattered to numerous thunderstorms develop during the afternoon and evening once again.  Several of the storms became strong and produced non-severe wind gusts and hail.  Isolated storms reached severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Meriwether county sheriff's deputy reported that several trees were down in the vicinity of Temple-Dukes Road in the southern part of the county.  A barn was also damaged in the vicinity.  An apparent downburst was evident on radar data in this area.  Doppler radar base velocities were as high as 50 knots between Warm Springs and Raleigh at 606 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance continues to move northeast across the area on the northwest side of a subtropical ridge which had moved southward across north Florida.  A hot, humid, very unstable air mass remained in place across the region.  Ample tropical moisture was also in place at the surface.  This provided the support for yet another afternoon and evening of scattered thunderstorms.  While the thunderstorms were not as numerous or as widespread as the previous day, several of the storms that did develop became strong to severe.  One storm produced a significant downburst across Lumpkin county downing over 200 trees and causing damage to homes, businesses, and schools across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Butts County Emergency Management Director reported that 20 trees were down in the vicinity of Haley Road near Worthville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper trough, lifting out of the mid-south, combined with deep moisture and an unstable air mass to generate several rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  A number of these thunderstorms became severe during the afternoon and evening producing large hail and damaging winds.  Morning rain and thunderstorms stabilized the air mass across the northern part of the state.  Thus, the majority of the severe thunderstorm events were across central Georgia, especially between Macon and Vidalia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that lightning struck a home on Summit Wood Drive resulting in a fire.  The home suffered major damage from the fire.  The lone occupant was able to escape without injury.

Radar data suggests that a strong thunderstorm downburst affected this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper low was moving from the mid-south into the southeast United States, while a deep upper trough and associated cold front was sweeping through the Ohio Valley into the northeast United States.  Deep tropical moisture remained across the southeast in advance of these systems.  Scattered thunderstorms developed across west central Georgia during the early afternoon and spread into the central and eastern parts of middle Georgia throughout the afternoon.  The main severe weather element on this day was damaging downburst winds.  Wind gusts of 63 and 82 mph were measured from Dodge and Houston counties, respectively, during severe thunderstorms.  One injury was reported from Laurens county from a falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public measured a wind gust of 82 mph at the intersection of Georgia Highway 127 and Moody Road, approximately one mile east-northeast of Houston Lake.  The public reported that dozens of trees were down in the Kathleen area.  Damage was also reported from the Warner Robins area where minor roof and siding damage was observed, a privacy fence was destroyed, and at least one vehicle was damaged by flying debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable and moist atmosphere was in place across the southeast United States.  The subtropical upper ridge that had been in place across the mid-south into the southeast had weakened and shifted slightly southeast of the region.  A weak upper trough was moving from the southern plains into the mid-Atlantic region.  This feature combined with the moist and unstable atmosphere was sufficient for isolated to scattered afternoon and evening convection on the 14th.  One storm in Butts county, just southeast of Atlanta, briefly became severe and produced damaging downburst winds.  Convection became even more widespread and intense on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Butts County Emergency Management Director reported that an apparent thunderstorm downburst from a small, yet severe thunderstorm had downed nearly a dozen trees approximately two miles west-southwest of McKibben in the western part of Butts county.  Most of the damage was near the junction of Interstate-75 and Georgia Highway 16.  Several trees were down on homes on Caldwell Road causing two homes to sustain minor roof damage.  The porch was ripped off one home on Caldwell Road as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough of low pressure aloft continued across the western Atlantic.  Deep north to north-northeast flow was noted aloft across the southeastern U.S.  A backdoor cold front was moving southwest into South Carolina and Georgia.  Considerable moisture and instability was present across the region as the front drifted southwest into northeast Georgia.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the region in advance of the front, then drifted southwest with the mean upper flow.  Several of the storms became severe producing quarter-sized hail and damaging downburst winds.  In addition, frequent to excessive cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with the storms.  At least one lightning-related fire was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Douglas County Sentinel reported that lightning struck the county's 911 Center tower rendering the severe weather warning sirens for the city inoperative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cutoff upper low remained over Alabama, but was weakening further and starting to become absorbed in the mean flow.  By May 31st, the upper low was lifting northeast toward the mid-Atlantic and exerting less influence on the weather across the area.  Scattered showers and thunderstorms once again developed in southeast to northwest oriented bands during the afternoon and evening.  As with previous days, a few of these became strong to severe and many produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public and a trained storm spotter observed penny to slightly larger than nickel-sized hail from the far southern part of Bibb county, south of Skipperton, to the central part of the county, just west of Macon.

The lightning was said to have struck his lower back and then traveled through his body coming out of his ear and the top of his shoulder blades.  He had to be revived three times as he was not breathing and had no pulse after the lightning strike.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local newspaper for the city of Eatonton reported that numerous trees were blown down in the vicinity of the Oconee Wildlife Management Agency.  Trees were down on a home, a vehicle, and at least one power line.  Most of the damage occurred in the vicinity of Village Court and Jamestown Drive.

A nursing home off U.S. Highway 41, approximately 1.5 miles south of Georgia Highway 96, suffered considerable roof damage.  A permanent awning was also torn off a home on St. John Avenue.  At least 25 trees were uprooted or snapped in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front was moving southwest from the mid-Atlantic region into the southeast U.S.  A hot, humid, unstable air mass remained in place in advance of the front, which helped to support the development of scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon.  Several of the storms across north Georgia became severe during the afternoon and producing damaging wind gusts.  Isolated strong thunderstorms also occurred across central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County 911 Center reported that nearly three dozen trees were down across the county.  Three homes and two vehicles were damaged from falling trees.  Several power lines were also down.  The hardest hit area was the Horseleg Creek area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County 911 Center and the Daily Tribune News of Cartersville reported that more than a dozen trees were down near Adairsville and at least eight trees were down near Red Top Mountain State Park in the far southeast part of the county near Lake Allatoona.  Several power lines were also down as a result.  The most significant damage was in the Oakland Heights area where several vehicles were damaged from downed trees.  A home on Cassville Road was damaged when a tree fell on the structure, which also damaged three adjacent vehicles.  The most significant damage was in the Oakland Heights area were several vehicles were damaged from downed trees.  In addition, a farm, also on Cassville Road, suffered damage when the roof was blown off a hay barn, several trees were blown down, and at least a couple of cows were injured or killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical ridge across the southeast U.S. continued to weaken and shift east of Georgia.  A strong upper trough, for early July, and associated cold front were approaching from the northwest.  A very warm, moist air mass remained in place across the southeast states in advance of these systems.  This provided ample support once again for scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon.  On this particular day, activity was generally confined to north central and northeast Georgia, mostly north of Interstate-20 and east of Interstate-75.  With the exception of one large hail event in northeast Georgia, all of the severe events on this day were in the form of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees were down from the northwest to the southeast part of the county.  Two trees were down on Hightower Road at Hightower Trail, two trees were down on Mineral Springs Road at Ballground Highway, two to three trees were down on Franklin Goalmine Road at Lower Creighton Road and Edwards Mill Road and two trees were down on Salacoa Road in the far northwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An weak upper trough continued to move from the southern plains toward the mid-Atlantic region.  A very warm and unstable air mass remained in place across the southeast United States.  Afternoon temperatures in the middle to upper 90s and deep moisture created a particularly unstable atmosphere across Georgia.  Convective Available Potential Energy (CAPE) values rose to in excess of 3000 J/KG during the afternoon.  Consequently, scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed, many of which became strong to severe.  Damaging downburst winds were noted with many of the storms.  As the storms moved into east Georgia, several of the storms produced quarter to golf ball-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Butts County Emergency Management Director reported that more than a dozen trees were down in the eastern part of the county, east of Jackson southward toward Flovilla.  Several roads around Flovilla were blocked by downed trees and had to be closed.  Roads affected included Higgins Road, Riley Road, and Pratt Smith Road in the Pine View subdivision, as well as Georgia Highways 16 and 42.
