EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Catoosa County Emergency Management Director reported that 12 trees were blown down southwest of Ringgold.  One tree fell on a home and another fell on a vehicle.  A barn was also damaged by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical ridge that had persisted across the southern U.S. was shifting further east off the southeast U.S. coast.  A deep upper trough was moving through the Ohio Valley.  The tail end of this trough was moving through the mid-south and into the southeast U.S.  With a hot, very moist air mass remaining in place scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon.  The most concentrated area of coverage was across west central into middle Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours when a mesoscale convective complex evolved and moved east-southeast across the area.  Widespread damaging wind gusts were observed as the complex tracked from near Columbus toward Vidalia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pulaski County Emergency Management Director reported that two buildings at an irrigation company off U.S. Highway 129, two miles south of Hawkinsville, were damaged from thunderstorm downburst winds.  A pontoon was pushed approximately 100 yards into one of these buildings.  Anywhere from six to eight trees and a few power lines and phone lines were also down in this same general area.

Radar data suggests that quarter and larger-sized hail likely fell in many areas between Helen and Sautee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was present over the Southeast. A weak lee-side trough developed over the Midlands during the afternoon and spawned showers and thunderstorms along residual convective outflows from the previous day's thunderstorm activity. Thunderstorms then shifted over portions of southeast Georgia during the early evening hours. An inland moving sea breeze prolonged shower and thunderstorms through the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported a 44 inch diameter tree blown down on Highway 17 near Blue Jay Road. A small truck hit the tree after it had fallen. The driver was killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the evening hours as an inland moving sea breeze interacted with differential heating boundaries. A few multi-cell thunderstorms became strong with damaging winds and heavy rainfall as they entered an environment that had surface based CAPE values around 3000 j/kg and DCAPE values over 800 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported sections of Cotton Avenue, North Masonic Street and Park Street in Millen closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper trough, lifting out of the mid-south, combined with deep moisture and an unstable air mass to generate several rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  A number of these thunderstorms became severe during the afternoon and evening producing large hail and damaging winds.  Morning rain and thunderstorms stabilized the air mass across the northern part of the state.  Thus, the majority of the severe thunderstorm events were across central Georgia, especially between Macon and Vidalia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bleckley County 911 Center, along with the public and amateur radio operators, reported that several trees were down across the county.  At least nine trees were down north of Cochran on Red Dog Farm Road and Long Street Road.  Other trees were down within the city of Cochran as well as on Ann Street, Annette Street, and Georgia Highway 26 West.  A railroad crossing arm on a Cochran street was broken in two as a result of the strong wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance continues to move northeast across the area on the northwest side of a subtropical ridge which had moved southward across north Florida.  A hot, humid, very unstable air mass remained in place across the region.  Ample tropical moisture was also in place at the surface.  This provided the support for yet another afternoon and evening of scattered thunderstorms.  While the thunderstorms were not as numerous or as widespread as the previous day, several of the storms that did develop became strong to severe.  One storm produced a significant downburst across Lumpkin county downing over 200 trees and causing damage to homes, businesses, and schools across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milton Herald reported that a large mansion home on Tullgean Drive was completely destroyed by a lightning strike.  A firefighter was injured while fighting the fire when the roof collapsed on him.  He suffered first and second degree burns on his legs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad high pressure aloft continued across the southeastern states and was beginning to strengthen from previous days.  However, a very weak disturbance was evident across the north side of the high across north Georgia.  This was sufficient given strong heating to produce isolated to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  A few of these became strong and produced pea-sized hail and non-severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck a home on Biltmore Woods Drive just east of Buford.  The resulting fire was contained to an exterior wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper trough continued to deepen from the Ohio Valley into the southeast U.S.  An associated cold front was moving into north Georgia.  Warm, moist, unstable air remained across Georgia.  The front provided additional support for scattered strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Upper support was best across the eastern half of the state.  Hence, most of the severe thunderstorms occurred in this section of the state.  Cloud-to-ground Lightning during evening thunderstorms in Henry county, just southeast of Atlanta, claimed the life of a 14-year old male and injured a 15-year old female.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sumter County Emergency Management Director reported that four pecan trees were down in a pecan orchard.  Several large tree branches were also down in the orchard as well on other trees.  One nearby building had damaged shingles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fox 5 Television of Atlanta relayed a report from a viewer of quarter-sized hail near Cleveland.  A separate report of penny-sized hail was received from the public in the Cleveland area as well.

Trees were down on Danielsvlle Road, Freeman Drive, and near the intersection of North Church Road and Smokey Road.  At least two power lines were also down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Emergency Management Director reported that two trees were blown down from strong winds caused by thunderstorm outflow well north of the actual thunderstorms across the southern part of the county and counties immediately to the south.  The trees also brought down a power line which ignited a small wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough was shifting slowly east from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A leading cold front was located from New York to Alabama.  A summerlike subtropical ridge across the southeast U.S. was slowly shifting off the southeast U.S. coast.  The highly meridional flow had allowed an unusually deep tropical air mass to spread far northward across the eastern half of the U.S.  This weather system had been responsible for widespread catastrophic flooding in western and middle Tennessee during the previous three days.  As the system moved toward Georgia, it began to weaken considerably and the main dynamics lifted northeast more toward the mid-Atlantic.  Nonetheless, the slow movement of the system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to the area with two-day rainfall of 3-4 inches.  Flash flooding was observed in several counties on the northwest and west side of Atlanta, some of the same counties that experienced catastrophic flooding during late September 2009.  Flash flooding during this event was far less significant.  Severe weather was isolated and confined to east central Georgia toward the end of the event on the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was observed along several creeks in Gwinnett county as indicated by USGS stream gages.  Crooked Creek at Spalding Drive, three miles northwest of Norcross, reached its flood stage of 11.0 feet at 705 am EDT.  The creek crested at 13.1 feet at 915 am EDT.  Pew Creek at Paterson Road reached its flood stage of 11.0 feet at 805 am EDT and crested at 11.8 feet at 915 am EDT.  Sweetwater Creek at Club Drive reached its flood stage of 11.0 feet at 720 am EDT, then crested at 12.6 feet at noon EDT.  Several roads along the creeks were closed for several hours because of the flooding.  Water covered the grounds of the driving range at the Sugarloaf Golf Facility in Duluth.  Flood damage in Gwinnett county was primarily limited to debris removal and estimated to be $10,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dooly County Emergency Management Director relayed several reports of penny-sized hail from the southern half of the county.  Hail was reported in Drayton, west of Vienna, and in Richwood.  This hail fell from one of three thunderstorms that tracked across the county.  The storm that tracked across the northern part of the county produced golf ball-sized hail and is documented separately.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lumpkin County Emergency Management Director reported that a significant thunderstorm downburst had tracked across Lumpkin county causing extensive damage.  Wind gusts were measured at the 911 Center in Dahlonega at 79 mph during this event.  More than 200 trees were blown down or uprooted across the county.  The downed trees and wind gusts caused extensive damage to property.  Several homes sustained minor to moderate damage.  Damage also occurred to two schools, a 6th grade academy and the Mount Education Center.  Both sustained roof and awning damage.  Four businesses also suffered structural damage.  Several roads across the county were blocked from downed trees.  More than 3000 residents of the county were without power for much of the night.||In addition, a large oak tree fell on a sewer line within Madeleine K. Anthony Park, causing raw sewage to spill into the Yahoola Creek.

Upper support was best across the eastern half of the state.  Hence, most of the severe thunderstorms occurred in this section of the state.  Cloud-to-ground Lightning during evening thunderstorms in Henry county, just southeast of Atlanta, claimed the life of a 14-year old male and injured a 15-year old female.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that a 14-year old male was struck by lightning while standing outside his home in the Huntington Ridge subdivision in McDonough.  The boy died overnight in the hospital following the incident.  A 15-year old girl was simultaneously injured by the same lightning strike, but survived and was said to be in good condition the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperature and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of south-central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index peaked at 111 degrees at the Valdosta airport at 7 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperature and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of south-central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index peaked at 113 degrees at the Valdosta airport at 1 pm EDT and remained above 110 degrees through 3 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong cold front was pushing south through the Ohio Valle and mid-Atlantic region and into the southeast U.S.  A large subtropical ridge of high pressure aloft was located across the southern plains.  This resulted in an unstable north to northwest flow aloft across the southeast states.  The upper support and frontal system combined with a hot, humid, unstable air mass remaining across Georgia to result in scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  A couple of the thunderstorms became severe and produced damaging wind gusts.  All of the thunderstorms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  One of these lightning strikes resulted in the second and third lightning-related deaths of the year for the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Haralson County Emergency Management Director and the county 911 Center reported that three trees were down across the southern part of the county.  A couple of phone lines were also down as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bibb County Emergency Management Agency reported that over two dozen trees were down from the northwest part of the county, north and northwest of Lake Tobesofkee southeast across the county to south and south-southeast of Macon.  At least a dozen trees were down within the city of Macon as well.  A Skywarn Spotter measured a wind gust of 62 mph one mile south of the intersection of Eisenhower Parkway and Pio Nono Avenue, approximately one mile south of downtown Macon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid, unstable air mass remained across the southeastern United States.  The region was located in a weakness between two subtropical upper ridges, one across the western Atlantic and the other stretching from Texas to the desert southwest.  An upper trough was moving through the Ohio Valley into the northeast U.S. with a weak accompanying cold front.  Upper support combined with the unstable air mass to result in scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms once again.  The majority of the thunderstorms were across north Georgia on this day, generally north of Interstate-20.  Many of these storms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning, producing at least one house fire in Gwinnett county, adding to the growing list of lightning-related fires this summer season.  With weak steering currents in place aloft, the storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain resulting in a flash flood event along Big Creek near Cumming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fox 5 Television of Atlanta and the Gwinnett Daily Post reported that lightning struck a home on Roberts Street just northeast of Buford.  The single story, single family home was completely destroyed as a result of the ensuing fire.  A family of four was displaced as a result of this incident, but no injuries occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short-lived severe thunderstorm developed during the afternoon hours in association with an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WCTV relayed a report of a downburst destroying a greenhouse on Coffee Road near Hahira.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong cold front was approaching Georgia from the northwest as the subtropical ridge retrograded west into Texas.  A disturbance in the increasingly stronger northwest flow aloft combined with the unstable, hot surface air mass to support the development of widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Many of the thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts downing numerous trees and power lines, especially across north Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Monroe County 911 Center reported that four trees were down in the southeast part of the county, southeast of Forsyth.

Her 14-year old cousin died on July 21st after being hospitalized in critical condition and on a respirator since the lightning strike on the 13th.

The thunderstorms produced intense cloud-to-ground lightning.  Some of the storms became strong and produced penny-sized hail and downburst winds, below severe criteria, however.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that yet a third home near Kennesaw was struck and set on fire by lightning.  This home was located on Stilesboro Drive.  Again, no specific details were provided regrading damages to the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southeast United States and mid-Atlantic region remained in a weakness area between two strong subtropical upper ridges of high pressure, one located in the western Atlantic and another in the desert southwest.  A weak trough was noted in between these two highs from the Ohio Valley into the southeast U.S.  With a hot, humid, unstable air mass remaining in place and unstable northwest flow aloft, albeit weak, scattered thunderstorms were the rule once again during the afternoon and evening.  Some of these became strong to briefly severe.  However, the biggest problem on this date was flooding and heavy rain.  One thunderstorm near Cartersville in Bartow county dumped three to four inches of rain in two hours, causing considerable street flooding, flooding basements of homes and one business, and resulting in a roof collapse of a specialty store.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fox 5 Television of Atlanta, along with other Atlanta broadcast media and the Cartersville Daily Tribune reported that heavy rain flooded several downtown Cartersville businesses as well as causing a partial roof collapse of the Hobby Lobby store at the Market Square Shopping Center.  Three to four inches of rain had fallen in a small area around downtown Cartersville during the previous two hours.  Considerable damage was reported to the store building and its contents, many of which were destroyed by rain water.  However, no injuries were reported as a result of the incident.||Several other downtown businesses suffered water damage when several inches of water crept into the structures, including Four Way Lunch, Swheat Market, City Cellar, and Moore's Market.  Several nearby homes on Herring Street were flooded with 2.5 to 3.0 feet of water.  Seven residents of these homes were rescued from the flooded homes.  Finally, the Keith Building, a city building, on Leake Street sustained significant water damage when water rose nearly two feet deep in the lower level of the structure.  Minor flooding was also observed at the building housing the Cartersville Daily Tribune.||Most of the flooding was determined to be the result of a clogged drainage ditch near the CSX Railroad tracks.  This prevented the heavy runoff water from reaching the nearby Etowah River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong cold front was approaching Georgia from the northwest as the subtropical ridge retrograded west into Texas.  A disturbance in the increasingly stronger northwest flow aloft combined with the unstable, hot surface air mass to support the development of widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Many of the thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts downing numerous trees and power lines, especially across north Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that well over a dozen trees were snapped off approximately 15 feet above the ground or uprooted in eastern and southeast Paulding county.  Most of the downed or damaged trees were in the Morningside Drive, Morris Road, South Lake Drive, Harper's Lake Drive, and Cleburne Parkway areas.  Public estimated that the damage was the result of a thunderstorm downburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across north and northeast Georgia while another stronger front approached from the Ohio Valley.  The subtropical ridge was building northward across the southern plains and mid-south.  A typical hot, humid, and unstable air mass remained in place across Georgia.  A complex of thunderstorms had moved across north Georgia during the late morning and early afternoon, leaving a significant cool outflow boundary draped near the Interstate-20 corridor by early afternoon. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms develop along and south of this outflow boundary during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many of these storms produced very heavy rain and frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  While none of the storms reached severe limits, one storm in Newnan blew down a tree, which caused damage to a Coweta county school building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Griffin Daily News reported that lightning struck the McDonough Road Baptist Church located at 1370 North McDonough Road.  The lightning struck the front gable, setting the attic on fire.  The resulting fire caused severe damage to the attic, foyer, and roof.  The rest of the building suffered minor smoke and water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical upper ridge persisted across the south central and southeast U.S. for several days from July 20th through July 28th.  Afternoon high temperatures across central Georgia, south of the fall line were mostly in the mid to upper 90s during this period, with a few readings near 100.  With a very moist, tropical air mass in place, dewpoints were unusually high, mostly in the mid 70s, with even a few upper 70s.  This pushed afternoon heat index values to near 110 across parts of central Georgia.  The high humidity did not allow for much cooling at night, with lows near 80 in these same areas.  The extended and oppressive heat resulted in the first heat-related deaths in the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area since the hot summer of 2007.  An elderly couple in Bleckley county died of heat exhaustion on July 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Macon Telegraph reported that an elderly couple passed away at their home of 62-years as the result of heat exposure or heat exhaustion.  The 90-year old male and 83-year old female couple left their home around 1030 am EDT Friday, 07/23/10, to go outside to a wooded area behind their home, approximately 50 yards away from the house.  They were both found dead by their son approximately 24 hours later still adjacent to their vehicle were they had gone the previous morning to discard some kitchen scraps.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Times Georgian of Carrollton reported that lightning struck a propane tank at Lyle Enterprises near Carrollton.  The lightning strike caused an explosion which set a 2400 square foot workshop on fire.  This fire in turn set an adjacent mobile home, that was used for storage, four trucks, a tractor, some lawn mowers, and other miscellaneous supplies on fire.  All of this property was destroyed as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While a strong subtropical ridge persisted across most of the southern United States, the remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five (TD5) continued to circulated around the subtropical ridge.  With the ridge at this time centered near New Orleans, the remnants of tropical depression number five had once again moved into Georgia.  Meanwhile...a weak frontal system had stalled across north Georgia, being pushed southward by a short wave moving through the Ohio Valley.  The combination of the front the remnants of TD5 resulted in numerous thunderstorms.  While none of the thunderstorms became severe, frequent lightning and very heavy rain accompanied many of the storms.  Localized flash flooding was reported in Cherokee county in north central Georgia during this event.  By far, however, the biggest issue on this day was frequent and intense cloud-to-ground lightning, which caused a rash of house fires in several northeast and east central Georgia counties.  The thunderstorms persisted into the early morning hours of the 21st across northeast Georgia between Athens, Lawrenceville, and Commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Athens Banner Herald reported that lightning struck a home on Antrim Glen Road.  The structure suffered moderate fire and smoke damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary remained stalled across central Georgia.  A broad low pressure trough was exiting the eastern U.S.  A strong subtropical ridge was on the verge of building from the Ohio Valley into the northeast U.S. during this period.  With the upper trough exiting and weakening across the eastern U.S., the frontal boundary remained stalled and eventually washed out.  This left the air mass very moist and unstable across the region.  Residual tropical moisture remained across the region, thanks largely to the lingering remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five.  As a result, scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across north Georgia and persisted into late evening across the central part of he state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pickens and Cherokee County Emergency Management Directors report that a strong thunderstorm downburst had downed nearly 100 trees near the Cherokee county, near and just north of Tate.  At least nine power poles were also down, including one main power transmission line.  Several roads were blocked from downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong backdoor cold front for the end of July was moving slowly southward through east and central Georgia.  Unstable northwest flow was present aloft around the eastern periphery of a strong subtropical ridge centered over north Texas.  A very hot, humid air mass remained in place across much of the state, but especially central Georgia south of the front.  This along with the added upper-level support provided ample support for scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorm development.  However, the most significant thunderstorm activity occurred during the late afternoon and evening when a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northeast Georgia between Gwinnett and Clarke counties and moved southeast toward Hancock and Jefferson counties before diminishing.  This thunderstorm complex produced damaging downburst winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walton County 911 Center reported that at least a dozen trees were down across the eastern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five (TD5) were drifting into the region from the southwest around a subtropical ridge located over north Florida.  The deep tropical moisture combined with a backdoor cold front across northeast Georgia to generate scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms across north and especially northeast Georgia.  The thunderstorms were very efficient rain producers and were also accompanied by intense cloud-to-ground lightning.  Damaging downburst winds and several lightning incidents were reported with the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clarke County 911 Center reported that lightning set a commercial business on fire on Voyles Road, approximately two miles northwest of Winterville in northeast Clarke county.  The business suffered significant damage from the resulting fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary remained stalled across central Georgia.  A broad low pressure trough was exiting the eastern U.S.  A strong subtropical ridge was on the verge of building from the Ohio Valley into the northeast U.S. during this period.  With the upper trough exiting and weakening across the eastern U.S., the frontal boundary remained stalled and eventually washed out.  This left the air mass very moist and unstable across the region.  Residual tropical moisture remained across the region, thanks largely to the lingering remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that the same thunderstorm downburst that began just north of Nelson in Pickens county continued south into extreme northern Cherokee county before dissipating.  More than a dozen trees were down in the extreme northern part of the county north of Ball Ground, near the Pickens county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, very moist, tropical air mass remained in place across the region.  The persistent subtropical ridge had shifted just to the southeast of Georgia.  A cold front was near the Georgia-Tennessee border.  The front, when combined with daytime heating and the very unstable, hot air mass, provided the necessary lift for widespread and numerous showers and thunderstorms.  It turned out to be the most active day for severe thunderstorms during the month of July.  Many of the storms produced damaging downburst wind gusts, marginally severe hail, and excessive cloud-to-ground lightning.  Flash flooding was also observed with a couple of the storms.  One early morning isolated storm over Coweta county, just southwest of Atlanta, produced three to four inches of rain in a couple of hours as it persisted over the same areas near Newnan in the very early morning hours after midnight.  Flash flooding was again observed later in the day across Gwinnett county northeast of Atlanta.||Lightning was particularly intense with the thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 26th.  Gwinnett county emergency management reported 14 house fires, one business fire, and two apartment fires between 430 pm and 8 pm EDT.||In addition to the severe thunderstorms and intense lightning, very hot conditions continued across the area, especially central Georgia.  High temperatures in parts of central Georgia reached or exceeded 100 degrees, with heat index values nearing 110 during the afternoon.  The intense heat took the life of yet another individual in central Georgia, this time in Twiggs county.  A 47-year old female died on her unshaded front porch just outside Jeffersonville, where the temperature at noon was observed to be 99 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny to nickel-sized hail in the southeast part of the county, four to five miles south of Nicholson.  Two trees were also blown down near the intersection of U.S. Highway 441 and Georgia Highway 334.

It was further noted that a field of corn was flattened by the strong winds.

The power line was down southwest of Canton along Bells Ferry Road.  A wind gust of 59 mph was measured on weather equipment located at the Emergency Operations Center in Canton.

Damaging downburst winds and several lightning incidents were reported with the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clarke County 911 Center reported that lightning struck an apartment complex approximately three miles northeast of Athens on Rolling Ridge Drive.  Damage from the lightning-related fire was minor to moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary, backdoor front lingered across northeast Georgia.  The remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five (TD5) were making one final revolution through Georgia around a large subtropical ridge across the mid-south.  Deep tropical moisture remained firmly entrenched across the state.  In fact, the air mass remained incredibly moist with dewpoints in the mid to upper 70s across much of north and central Georgia.  Thunderstorms, with heavy rain, had plagued northeast Georgia throughout the early morning and mid-morning hours and persisted across the far northeastern counties into the early afternoon.  By late morning, rainfall totals of six inches, locally more, were common across the northwest half of White county.  The heavy rainfall in the steep terrain common to this area resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White County Emergency Management Director reported significant flash flooding near and just south of Cleveland.  The most significant incident occurred along a service road of U.S. Highway 129, approximately one mile south of Cleveland.  Significant erosion, six to eight feet deep in spots, occurred in the area resulting in a pavement collapse.  The collapsed pavement resulted in a sink hole causing damage to a car wash and adjacent Sonic Drive In restaurant along U.S. Highway 129.  Most of the damage was in the form of collapsed awnings and damage to adjacent property at the drive in.  In addition, Cox Creek overflowed its banks onto U.S. Highway 129 near Campbell Street with four feet of water and onto Quillian Street with two feet of water.  Freeman Creek overflowed its banks onto Asbestos Road with one foot of water.  A secondary stream that flows into Cox Creek overflowed its banks flooding Cementary Street and Wildord Ash Parkway with one foot of water.  Portions of the Creekside Deli Parking Lot at Nachoochee Road and U.S. Highway 129 were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid, unstable air mass remained across the southeastern United States.  The region was located in a weakness between two subtropical upper ridges, one across the western Atlantic and the other stretching from Texas to the desert southwest.  An upper trough was moving through the Ohio Valley into the northeast U.S. with a weak accompanying cold front.  Upper support combined with the unstable air mass to result in scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms once again.  The majority of the thunderstorms were across north Georgia on this day, generally north of Interstate-20.  Many of these storms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning, producing at least one house fire in Gwinnett county, adding to the growing list of lightning-related fires this summer season.  With weak steering currents in place aloft, the storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain resulting in a flash flood event along Big Creek near Cumming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gage on Big Creek near Cumming (CMMG1) exceeded its flood stage of 6.0 feet at 1030 pm EDT.  The river continued to rise and reached 8.14 feet at 515 am EDT on July 17, 2010.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal adjacent to the creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across central Coweta county shortly just after midnight on July 25th and persisted around Newnan for two to three hours.  Three to four inches of rain fell in this area during this time frame resulting in flash flooding and flooding of streets and property.  Flash flooding was observed along several roads around Newnan, including McCullom-Sharpsburg Road/Georgia Highway 154, Thomason Road, East Broad Street, Jefferson Street, and Postal Parkway.  Walt Sanders Road, just off Georgia Highway 34, was closed with one to two feet of water over it as well.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal in the flooded areas.

The intense heat took the life of yet another individual in central Georgia, this time in Twiggs county.  A 47-year old female died on her unshaded front porch just outside Jeffersonville, where the temperature at noon was observed to be 99 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck a two-story wood frame home on Mill Bend Drive in Lawrenceville.  Firefighters found the attic and a bedroom on fire when they arrived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid, unstable air mass remained across the southeastern United States.  Despite strong high pressure aloft centered over the southeastern U.S., scattered, diurnal pulse thunderstorms developed once again across the entire area during afternoon heating and continued well into the evening.  The thunderstorms that did develop dissipated quickly.  However, with surface temperatures in the mid to upper 90s, as the thunderstorms collapsed they produced damaging downburst winds.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was noted with many of the thunderstorms.  Yet another lightning injury occurred from these afternoon thunderstorms, continuing a very dangerous summer from lightning-related incidents across north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Douglas County Emergency Manager, the Atlanta broadcast media, and the Douglas County Sentinel reported that a 15-year old male was struck by lightning while working with his father in the garage at their home on Bellemeade Drive off Coursey Lake Road.  The teenage boy was apparently pushing the garage door opener button to close the door when the lightning strike occurred.  The boy's arm was numb for several hours, but otherwise all injuries appeared to be minor.  He was transported to a local hospital, treated, and released.

The front, when combined with daytime heating and the very unstable, hot air mass, provided the necessary lift for widespread and numerous showers and thunderstorms.  It turned out to be the most active day for severe thunderstorms during the month of July.  Many of the storms produced damaging downburst wind gusts, marginally severe hail, and excessive cloud-to-ground lightning.  Flash flooding was also observed with a couple of the storms. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong summerlike subtropical ridge continued across the southeast U.S.  As a consequence, temperatures remained well above normal late into the month, with high temperatures in the 90s many days.  The western edge of the subtropical ridge was beginning to weaken during the last week of September, allowing moisture and better dynamics to creep into the western part of the state.  With the abnormally high temperatures for late September, isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed across west central and parts of central Georgia during the mid and late afternoon hours and continued into the evening hours.  The storms were brief, but did produce strong downburst wind gusts and frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  The storms generally dissipated after sunset, but isolated showers and thunderstorms redeveloped and spread into northwest Georgia during the late night and early morning hours, resulting in yet another lightning-related incident during the early morning hours of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Troup County 911 Center reported that lightning ignited a small grass fire in a field near Baughs Crossroads in southern Troup county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Harris County Emergency Management Director reported that thunderstorm downburst winds blew over a chicken coop.  The winds also blew in the front and back doors of a nearby barn.  The doors were closed prior to the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong cold front for early August was moving through the eastern U.S., including the southeast U.S., supported by a vigorous upper trough in the northeast U.S.  With a very moist, unstable air mass remaining in place at the surface across the southeast U.S., scattered strong and isolated severe thunderstorms developed across mainly north Georgia during the afternoon.  Most of the damage was the result of thunderstorm downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fannin County 911 Center reported that four trees were blown down across the eastern portion of the county, around Morganton on Old Georgia Highway 76.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five (TD5) were drifting into the region from the southwest around a subtropical ridge located over north Florida.  The deep tropical moisture combined with a backdoor cold front across northeast Georgia to generate scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms across north and especially northeast Georgia.  The thunderstorms were very efficient rain producers and were also accompanied by intense cloud-to-ground lightning.  Damaging downburst winds and several lightning incidents were reported with the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jackson County 911 Center reported that a portion of a roof was blown off one home and a chimney was blown off another home on Jameston Drive near Jefferson.  One tree was also down on Ralph Garrison Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant long period swells began along the Georgia coast as distant tropical storm Colin made a north shift well offshore. As swell began to increase over the area, persistent northeast surface winds along a Perigean spring tide further enhanced conditions for rip currents during the afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tybee Island lifeguards and Tybee Island Fire Department reported numerous rip currents being observed along the beach from 11 AM to 8 PM. There were 2 near-drownings and 24 additional rip current related rescues.  The two near-drownings occurred along the beach close to the intersection of 8th street and Bulter Avenue on Tybee Island. The Tybee Ocean Rescue Squad pulled both victims from the water and transported them to Memorial Medical Center.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across central Coweta county shortly just after midnight on July 25th and persisted around Newnan for two to three hours.  Three to four inches of rain fell in this area during this time frame resulting in flash flooding and flooding of streets and property.  Shenadoah Boulevard near the White Oak Golf Course was flooded with up to four feet of water flowing over the road.  Several other roads in the area were flooded with up to two feet of water over some of these as well.  A resident of Newnan reported that water was two feet deep in his yard on Prestwick Drive.  Flash flooding was observed along McCullom-Sharpsburg Road or Georgia Highway 154, Thomason Road, East Broad Street, Jefferson Street, and Postal Parkway.  Walt Sanders Road, just off Georgia Highway 34, was closed with one to two feet of water over it as well.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal in the flooded areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong backdoor cold front for the end of July was moving slowly southward through east and central Georgia.  Unstable northwest flow was present aloft around the eastern periphery of a strong subtropical ridge centered over north Texas.  A very hot, humid air mass remained in place across much of the state, but especially central Georgia south of the front.  This along with the added upper-level support provided ample support for scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorm development.  However, the most significant thunderstorm activity occurred during the late afternoon and evening when a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northeast Georgia between Gwinnett and Clarke counties and moved southeast toward Hancock and Jefferson counties before diminishing.  This thunderstorm complex produced damaging downburst winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sumter County Emergency Management Director reported that hail of penny, perhaps slightly larger, fell in the southwest part of the county near Croxton Crossroads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong summerlike subtropical ridge continued across the southeast U.S.  As a consequence, temperatures remained well above normal late into the month, with high temperatures in the 90s many days.  The western edge of the subtropical ridge was beginning to weaken during the last week of September, allowing moisture and better dynamics to creep into the western part of the state.  With the abnormally high temperatures for late September, isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed across west central and parts of central Georgia during the mid and late afternoon hours and continued into the evening hours.  The storms were brief, but did produce strong downburst wind gusts and frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  The storms generally dissipated after sunset, but isolated showers and thunderstorms redeveloped and spread into northwest Georgia during the late night and early morning hours, resulting in yet another lightning-related incident during the early morning hours of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported damage to a light fixture in a home caused by a lightning strike.  Other non-specific minor damage was also observed as a result of the lightning strike.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that three homes in unincorporated Cherokee county were struck by lightning and sustained minor to moderate damage.  The public reported that a 60 foot live oak tree at the Department of Education building was struck by lightning.  The lightning strike caused the tree to fall on an adjacent Nissan Xterra, crushing the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front lingered from southeast into west central Georgia.  The remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five (TD5) remained across the region as well.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed near the front during the afternoon and evening hours in the warm, moist, unstable air mass residing across the state.  While no severe thunderstorms were observed on this day, cloud-to-ground lightning was frequent and intense.  A lightning strike from a thunderstorm in Emanuel county destroyed an old schoolhouse and another lightning strike injured a U.S. Postal carrier near Macon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Macon Telegraph reported that lightning struck a U.S. Postal Service mail carrier while placing mail in a mailbox on Rocky Creek Road in Macon.  The female mail carrier was taken to the hospital after asking for medical attention, but did not sustain any life-threatening injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Savannah Morning News reported that lightning struck the high school wing of the David Emanuel Academy near Stillmore in rural southeast Emanuel county.  The two-story building, which was constructed in 1904, was set on fire by the lightning strike and completely destroyed.  The fire destroyed a science lab, classroom, and wrestling gym.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the northwest became stalled across northwest Georgia as a strong subtropical ridge of high pressure built northwestward from the Atlantic.  Persistent deep tropical moisture was present across the region as the remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five (TD5) continued to circle through the southeast and mid-south region on the west side of the subtropical ridge.  An area of showers and thunderstorms persisted during the morning and into the early afternoon from northwest Alabama into far northwest Georgia and eastern Tennessee.  A very small localized area of rainfall in excess of six inches was observed near the Tennessee border in northern Murray county, which resultant flash flooding along the Jacks and Conasauga Rivers in the Alaculsy Valley Campground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The United States Forest Service reported flash flooding near the merging point of the Jacks River and the Conasauga River and several smaller creeks, streams, and tributaries.  Several campers and hikers were evacuated from the Alaculsy Valley Campground within the Cohutta Wildlife Management Agency.  Minor flooding was also observed along portions of Old Georgia Highway 2 and Cable Road to the west near Cisco.  Damage was confined to debris removal and minor erosion to a few secondary dirt roads.  The heavy rain and erosion also caused a few trees to fall on minor, secondary roads within the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marietta Daily Journal reported that lightning struck a home on Helga Way between Kennesaw and Woodstock in northeast Cobb county.  The home was severely damaged from the fire which took at least 30 minutes to contain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical ridge that had been over the area for several days, was beginning to break down and shift east and south of the area.  This was allowing a cold front to approach from the north.  In addition, an subtropical low was evident across Texas and this was slowing drifting northeast.  A hot, unstable, and very moist tropical atmosphere remained across the region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed quickly during the afternoon across north Georgia, generally from Rome, to Canton, to Gainesville and northward.  The added synoptic support in comparison to previous days resulted in an upswing in the intensity and coverage of storms across this area.  Many of the storms became strong and a few became severe producing damaging downburst winds and large hail.  All of the storms produced frequent, intense cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County 911 Center relayed reports of nickel to golf ball-sized hail from the northwest part of the county between Adairsville and the Floyd and Gordon county lines.  The large hail broke windows of homes in Adairsville.  A couple of trees were also down from thunderstorms winds.  One tree was down on Jarrett Loop Road and another on White Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that several trees were blown down around Eatonton.  A portion of U.S. 441 Business Highway in the city of Eatonton was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge persisted across Texas, but was beginning to build wastward into the mid-south and southeast U.S.  An upper trough was located across the northeast into the mid-Atlantic region.  This provided Georgia with unstable northwest flow aloft.  Meanwhile...a weak cold front was moving slowly south through central Georgia.  Hot temperatures and dewpoints in the low and mid 70s made for a very unstable atmosphere across the region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the mid-afternoon and persisted into the evening.  The most persistent and intense thunderstorms originated from a cluster of thunderstorms that developed in northeast Georgia and slowly worked its way south-southwest into central Georgia.  Intense lightning and damaging downburst winds were noted with some of the thunderstorms, along with very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Douglas County Emergency Management Director reported that a home in Fairplay was struck by lightning.  Damage to the home was in the form of a damaged phone box and the electrical wiring adjacent to a clothes dryer in the utility room.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While a strong subtropical ridge persisted across most of the southern United States, the remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five (TD5) continued to circulated around the subtropical ridge.  With the ridge at this time centered near New Orleans, the remnants of tropical depression number five had once again moved into Georgia.  Meanwhile...a weak frontal system had stalled across north Georgia, being pushed southward by a short wave moving through the Ohio Valley.  The combination of the front the remnants of TD5 resulted in numerous thunderstorms.  While none of the thunderstorms became severe, frequent lightning and very heavy rain accompanied many of the storms.  Localized flash flooding was reported in Cherokee county in north central Georgia during this event.  By far, however, the biggest issue on this day was frequent and intense cloud-to-ground lightning, which caused a rash of house fires in several northeast and east central Georgia counties.  The thunderstorms persisted into the early morning hours of the 21st across northeast Georgia between Athens, Lawrenceville, and Commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported flash flooding along Byrd Creek, five miles north-northwest of Canton, and along Shoal Creek, one mile west-northwest of Waleska.  At least three feet of water from Byrd Creek was overflowing Sardis Circle north-northwest of Canton, and approximately a foot of water was flowing over George Gray Lane, just west-northwest of Waleska.  Minor erosion to the roads was observed, along with subsequent debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The eastern U.S. upper trough noted on the 26th of September continued to evolve into a deep upper low across the southeast U.S. with an associated pocket of cold temperatures aloft.  Temperatures aloft at 500mb were near -20C during the late afternoon and early evening hours of September 27th.  As the preceding area of rain and thunderstorms exited the area to the east during the afternoon, cloudy conditions across north and west Georgia gave way to partly sunny skies.  This allowed temperatures to warm into the low and mid 80s across the region.  The warm surface temperatures created a very unstable environment as the upper low and cold core pocket rotated across the region.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across north Georgia, with lesser coverage of convective activity across central Georgia.  Only the southwest part of the county warning area managed to escape the afternoon thunderstorms.  The thunderstorms were fast moving, but several produced damaging downburst winds and penny to occasionally golf ball-sized hail.  A strong shower on the tail end of the convective activity (no lightning observed) even produced a brief tornado in Laurens county in southeast Georgia.  The severe weather event only lasted a couple of hours in the 530 pm to 730 pm time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of hail, ranging in size from pennies to quarters, were received from the southeast and east central parts of Cobb county from just northwest of Mableton to just east of Smyrna.  Quarter-sized hail was observed near Mableton and Smyrna, with penny-sized hail just east of Smyrna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long term drought created dry surface fuels across southeast Georgia. The Arabia Bay Wildfire was first reported to the NWS in Jacksonville on November 10th in northern Clinch county. The fire started in a peat swamp and grew to approximately 500 acres in size by November 14th.  The Arabia Bay fire grew to 2710 acres by the end of the month and was ongoing but 100% contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Arabia Bay wildfire burned within a peat swamp for most of the month of November. The fire grew from 500 acres on Nov. 14th to 700 acres on the 15th. On the 19th, the fire was approximately 1170 acres. On the 23rd the fire had grown to 2688 acres and was about 75% contained. By the 25th, the fire was 100% contained and had reached 2710 acres in size. The fire had produced significant amounts of smoke over a large portion of southern Georgia and north Florida. This smoke and fog indirectly resulted in an injury to a Sheriff's Officer due to a traffic accident.||Heavy ash and smoke were reported near the fire, and depending on the wind regime, some days very heavy smoke impacted Atkinson and Bacon counties  (north of the fire site). Smoke was the major impact from the fire as property was not threatened. There were no injuries reported to the NWS that were fire related. There were several road closures due to smoke near the fire. ||The fire burned through the end of the month and into December. The NWS Jacksonville provided daily site specific fire weather forecasts, spot forecasts and ran Hysplit runs and disseminated this data to the GEMA Area Coordinator as well as created a web site for fire response officials to reference. ||On Nov. 23rd, the total cost spent on fire control was $230,672. This amount does not include property damage to swampland.

A strong shower on the tail end of the convective activity (no lightning observed) even produced a brief tornado in Laurens county in southeast Georgia.  The severe weather event only lasted a couple of hours in the 530 pm to 730 pm time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong cold front for early August was moving through the eastern U.S., including the southeast U.S., supported by a vigorous upper trough in the northeast U.S.  With a very moist, unstable air mass remaining in place at the surface across the southeast U.S., scattered strong and isolated severe thunderstorms developed across mainly north Georgia during the afternoon.  Most of the damage was the result of thunderstorm downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County Emergency Management Director reported that a few trees were blown down in the west central and northwest part of the county near Mountaintown.  Event times and location were based on radar data.  The Emergency Manager reported that approximately 10 trees were blown down across the county in an approximate one-hour period between 6 pm and 7 pm EDT.  Radar data suggests that much of this occurred near Tails Creek in the west central part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While a strong subtropical ridge persisted across most of the southern United States, the remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five (TD5) continued to circulated around the subtropical ridge.  With the ridge at this time centered near New Orleans, the remnants of tropical depression number five had once again moved into Georgia.  Meanwhile...a weak frontal system had stalled across north Georgia, being pushed southward by a short wave moving through the Ohio Valley.  The combination of the front the remnants of TD5 resulted in numerous thunderstorms.  While none of the thunderstorms became severe, frequent lightning and very heavy rain accompanied many of the storms.  Localized flash flooding was reported in Cherokee county in north central Georgia during this event.  By far, however, the biggest issue on this day was frequent and intense cloud-to-ground lightning, which caused a rash of house fires in several northeast and east central Georgia counties.  The thunderstorms persisted into the early morning hours of the 21st across northeast Georgia between Athens, Lawrenceville, and Commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck a home on Hambrick Drive.  The lightning struck the attic area of the home.  The resulting fire caused extensive damage to the attic and moderate damage to the second floor of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered, pulse severe storms affected northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The awning was blown off a restaurant on highway 441, about a mile north of Demorest. Also, a large tree was uprooted on Demorest-Mt. Airy highway and a very large limb was snapped on Thomas St in Cornelia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved into northeast Georgia during the early evening hours. One of the storms produced some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down from the Batesville area to north of Clarkesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours. The front triggered thunderstorms over the southern mountains of North Carolina which then moved southeast, crossing much of northeast Georgia. Some of the storms produced areas of damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Jackson Bridge Rd about 3 miles east southeast of Carnesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five (TD5) were drifting into the region from the southwest around a subtropical ridge located over north Florida.  The deep tropical moisture combined with a backdoor cold front across northeast Georgia to generate scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms across north and especially northeast Georgia.  The thunderstorms were very efficient rain producers and were also accompanied by intense cloud-to-ground lightning.  Damaging downburst winds and several lightning incidents were reported with the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clarke County 911 Center reported that at least 10 trees and a half-a-dozen power lines were down from northwest of Winterville to south of Athens near the Oconee county line.  One tree was down on Simonton Bridge Road, blocking the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough across the Ohio and Tennessee Valley was dropping south into the southeast U.S. and rapidly trending toward a deep, cold core upper low.  The first wave of inclement weather associated with this system occurred as an area of widespread moderate to heavy rain, with a line of strong thunderstorms along the leading edge.  While none of these thunderstorms became severe on the 26th, tornado watches were in effect for much of north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening and some storms along the leading edge of activity exhibited at least weak mesocyclones across east central Georgia.  A tornado warning was issued for a storm in southern Wilkes county during the late afternoon.  However, no tornado developed with this storm.  Widespread rainfall of two to three inches was common across east and south Georgia, with rainfall in excess of four inches in parts of Oglethorpe county.  Some flooding was observed in the Warner Robins area during the evening where rainfall of three to four inches was common.  This was the first widespread rain for the area since late August, so flooding in general was not an issue, despite the heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Citizen and Georgian of Montezuma reported that a large tree was blown down onto a home on Green Street during a strong thunderstorm.  The tree left a whole in the house about the size of a watermelon.  Considerable damage was noted to the roof, the attic, and the laundry room as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front an upper-level trough were approaching the area from the northwest.  While the air mass across north and central Georgia had been relatively dry and stable for several days, low-level moisture was on the increase in advance of the front.  In addition, the remnants of Tropical Storm Hermine had become caught in the upper-level trough and front and contributed to an increase in thunderstorm activity across the region on this day.  A few of the storms became strong to briefly severe in north Georgia during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pickens County 911 Center reported that three trees and one power line were down in the far southern portion of the county between Talmadge and Tate near the Cherokee county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper trough that had lingered across the northeast and mid-Atlantic region was moving out and a large vertically stacked high pressure area was rapidly replacing the trough across the northeast and mid-Atlantic.  During the next few days, this high pressure area would drift south toward Georgia, finally shutting off the incessant thunderstorm activity.  However, deep tropical moisture lingered along and south of a stationary front, that during the early morning was draped west-to-east across north central and northeast Georgia.  The front served as a focusing mechanism for persistent redevelopment of thunderstorms.  Two rounds of thunderstorms, several severe, plagued the area on August 27th.  The first occurred across north central and northeast Georgia, near Atlanta and Athens, during the early morning hours between midnight and 4 am EDT.  The second round occurred during the mid to late afternoon mainly across east central.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter-sized hail near McDonough.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jackson County 911 Center relayed a report of quarter-sized hail from the far northeastern part of the county in the Maysville area along the Banks county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing line of storms produced a little small hail and downed a few trees over northeast Georgia. The line became stronger as it moved into Upstate South Carolina.
A developing line of storms produced a little small hail and downed a few trees over northeast Georgia. The line became stronger as it moved into Upstate South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down to the southwest of Eastanollee, including along Broad River Rd and Ridge Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms became anchored over the southeast slopes of the Appalachian Mountains during the afternoon hours. The storms produced very heavy rain which lead to a few instances of flash flooding over Rabun County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of highway 76 was closed due to flooding about 2 miles southeast of Clayton. Also, a bridge over Ramey Rd was flooded in the Lakemont community and a creek flooded a portion of Lake Rabun Rd about 8 miles south southwest of Clayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper trough that had lingered across the northeast and mid-Atlantic region was moving out and a large vertically stacked high pressure area was rapidly replacing the trough across the northeast and mid-Atlantic.  During the next few days, this high pressure area would drift south toward Georgia, finally shutting off the incessant thunderstorm activity.  However, deep tropical moisture lingered along and south of a stationary front, that during the early morning was draped west-to-east across north central and northeast Georgia.  The front served as a focusing mechanism for persistent redevelopment of thunderstorms.  Two rounds of thunderstorms, several severe, plagued the area on August 27th.  The first occurred across north central and northeast Georgia, near Atlanta and Athens, during the early morning hours between midnight and 4 am EDT.  The second round occurred during the mid to late afternoon mainly across east central.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed nickel to slightly larger than nickel-sized hail east of Loganville on Bay Creek Church Road.  The Walton County Emergency Management Director relayed several reports of pea-sized hail.  Radar data suggests that pea to nickel-size hail likely fell further west across the county as well, coincident with several reports of thunderstorm wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper trough that had lingered across the northeast and mid-Atlantic region was moving out and a large vertically stacked high pressure area was rapidly replacing the trough across the northeast and mid-Atlantic.  During the next few days, this high pressure area would drift south toward Georgia, finally shutting off the incessant thunderstorm activity.  However, deep tropical moisture lingered along and south of a stationary front, that during the early morning was draped west-to-east across north central and northeast Georgia. The first occurred across north central and northeast Georgia, near Atlanta and Athens, during the early morning hours between midnight and 4 am EDT.  The second round occurred during the mid to late afternoon mainly across east central.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Emergency Management Director reported that a strong thunderstorm downburst, wind gusts of 60 mph or greater, blew down dozens of trees and several power lines from northeast of Wrightsville to three miles west-southwest of Wrightsville.  Six homes across this path sustained minor to moderate damage from downed trees.  Several trees were down and a trampoline was blown away from a residence near U.S. Highway 319 on Tucker Grove Church Road west of Wrightsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front continued to approach Georgia from the northwest as a deep upper trough was rotating through the northeast U.S.  Unstable northwest flow aloft combined with the front and a very hot, humid air mass across Georgia to help develop scattered to numerous thunderstorms across the north part of the state during the afternoon and evening.  Only a couple of these thunderstorms became severe, but all produced frequent to intense cloud-to-ground lightning and very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Athens Banner Herald reported that lightning struck a tree next to a home on State Street in Commerce.  The tree subsequently fell onto the roof of the adjacent garage, causing damage to the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure aloft dominated the southeast and east central U.S. during much of September.  This resulted in well above average temperatures and little to no rainfall during this period.  Soils and vegetation became increasingly dry resulting in an increased threat for wildfires.  A minor human-caused wildfire was observed at Johns Mountain Overlook in Walker County on September 18th.  The fire burned 1/10 acre with negligible monetary damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported that a human-caused wildfire at South Dicks Ridge in Walker County burned 1/4 acre and caused $1000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep layer short wave trough moved across southeast Georgia in the afternoon and evening which enhanced convection along the sea breeze fronts and convective outflows. Dry air entrainment aloft increased down burst potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Waycross Journal/Herald reported a lightning fire near Waynesville that burned about a tenth of an acre before fire fighters were able to control the blaze. The cost of property damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The eastern U.S. upper trough noted on the 26th of September continued to evolve into a deep upper low across the southeast U.S. with an associated pocket of cold temperatures aloft.  Temperatures aloft at 500mb were near -20C during the late afternoon and early evening hours of September 27th.  As the preceding area of rain and thunderstorms exited the area to the east during the afternoon, cloudy conditions across north and west Georgia gave way to partly sunny skies.  This allowed temperatures to warm into the low and mid 80s across the region.  The warm surface temperatures created a very unstable environment as the upper low and cold core pocket rotated across the region.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across north Georgia, with lesser coverage of convective activity across central Georgia.  Only the southwest part of the county warning area managed to escape the afternoon thunderstorms.  The thunderstorms were fast moving, but several produced damaging downburst winds and penny to occasionally golf ball-sized hail.  A strong shower on the tail end of the convective activity (no lightning observed) even produced a brief tornado in Laurens county in southeast Georgia.  The severe weather event only lasted a couple of hours in the 530 pm to 730 pm time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jackson County 911 Center relayed a report of quarter-sized hail in the Maysville area long the Banks county line.  Radar data suggests that similar-size hail likely continued eastward toward Banks Crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The eastern U.S. upper trough noted on the 26th of September continued to evolve into a deep upper low across the southeast U.S. with an associated pocket of cold temperatures aloft.  Temperatures aloft at 500mb were near -20C during the late afternoon and early evening hours of September 27th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County 911 Center reported that a lightning strike caused a fire at the Gilmer County Water pump station in Ellijay.  One tree was also blown down in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A deputy with the Coweta County Sheriff's Office observed quarter-sized hail on Fischer Road between Thomas Crossroads and Cannongate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The eastern U.S. upper trough noted on the 26th of September continued to evolve into a deep upper low across the southeast U.S. with an associated pocket of cold temperatures aloft.  Temperatures aloft at 500mb were near -20C during the late afternoon and early evening hours of September 27th.  As the preceding area of rain and thunderstorms exited the area to the east during the afternoon, cloudy conditions across north and west Georgia gave way to partly sunny skies.  This allowed temperatures to warm into the low and mid 80s across the region.
