The severe weather event only lasted a couple of hours in the 530 pm to 730 pm time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported lots of pea to penny-sized hail in the Lithia Springs area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered supercell thunderstorms developed over northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours as strong wind shear and moderate instability overspread the region ahead of a strong cold front. A number of tornadoes were spawned by the storms in the Carolinas, but only a little wind damage was reported in northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down near the intersection of Walls Bridge Rd and New Liberty Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a weak lee-side trough over the eastern midlands.  The thunderstorms developed and organized into a line as they met up with the inland pushing sea breeze across South Carolina.  Additional thunderstorms developed late across southeast Georgia as outflow boundary collisions occurred in a moist and unstable atmosphere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported four trees down, one on Rocky Ford Road, another on Stoopto Road, another on Edenfied Road, and the last on Fire Tower Road.  The tree damage occurred approximately 6 miles west of Sylvania, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid airmass resulted in heat index values of 115 degrees or higher across much of coastal South Carolina and coastal Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat index of 119 degrees was measured by the mesonet station in Wilmington Island, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite strong subtropical high pressure remaining over the region, a hot, very moist, tropical air mass provided ample support for afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  An apparent very weak surface boundary provided additional weak focus for thunderstorms across north Georgia.  None of the storms reached severe criteria, but one storm in Cobb county did produce damaging wind gusts and resulting minor tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported that four 10-12 inch diameter trees were blown down or snapped-in-half along Moon Station Lane Northwest near Acworth.  It was also reported that several tree limbs and associated debris were scattered about the area.  WSB Television of Atlanta reported that another tree was blown down onto a home along Hickory Creek Court in Acworth.  The downed tree caused damage to the chimney and gutters as well as an adjacent Fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southeast United States and mid-Atlantic region remained in a weakness area between two strong subtropical upper ridges of high pressure, one located in the western Atlantic and another in the desert southwest.  A weak trough was noted in between these two highs from the Ohio Valley into the southeast U.S.  With a hot, humid, unstable air mass remaining in place and unstable northwest flow aloft, albeit weak, scattered thunderstorms were the rule once again during the afternoon and evening.  Some of these became strong to briefly severe.  However, the biggest problem on this date was flooding and heavy rain.  One thunderstorm near Cartersville in Bartow county dumped three to four inches of rain in two hours, causing considerable street flooding, flooding basements of homes and one business, and resulting in a roof collapse of a specialty store.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County Emergency Management Director reported that at least nine trees were down around Cartersville, some on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread surface heating and continued deep moisture advection ahead of an approaching cold front from the northwest allowed for very unstable atmospheric conditions with CAPE values between 3500 to 4000 J/kg Friday afternoon and evening. As a 500 mb shortwave tracked along the southern periphery of a broad through and across the region, severe thunderstorms developed across the area. Severe thunderstorms initially developed along the seabreeze in the area late in the afternoon. More organized severe storms tracked into the area from the Midlands during the evening and pushed all the way into portions of southeast Georgia during the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A electric company reported trees down on power lines between Rocky Ford and Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge was building westward from the western Atlantic into the southeast United States.  Warm air aloft, associated with the upper high, was resulting in an increasingly unfavorable environment for thunderstorm development.  However, with a hot, moist, unstable surface air mass remaining in place, the upper-level cap was not sufficient enough to prevent the typical diurnal pulse thunderstorms from developing, especially in far northwest Georgia, furthest removed from the upper high.  One of these thunderstorms briefly became severe near Chatsworth in Murray county and produced damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Murray County 911 Center reported that thunderstorm downburst winds blew down at least six trees southwest of Chatsworth near the intersection of Georgia Highway 225 and New Hope Church Road.  In addition, a portion of the roof of a home was ripped off.  Several large tree limbs were also down, falling on and causing damage to some vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary in combination with the sea breeze, resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree and power line down across the road near the intersection of Old Reidsville-Collins Road and Burkhalter Road, a tree down near the intersection of Lazy K Lane and Highway 178, and estimated winds of 60 mph, in Reidsville, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid, unstable air mass remained across the southeastern United States.  Despite strong high pressure aloft centered over the southeastern U.S., scattered, diurnal pulse thunderstorms developed once again across the entire area during afternoon heating and continued well into the evening.  The thunderstorms that did develop dissipated quickly.  However, with surface temperatures in the mid to upper 90s, as the thunderstorms collapsed they produced damaging downburst winds.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was noted with many of the thunderstorms.  Yet another lightning injury occurred from these afternoon thunderstorms, continuing a very dangerous summer from lightning-related incidents across north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Douglas County 911 Center reported that a tree on Bomar Road at Berry Farm Road was struck by lightning causing it to fall onto a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record heat across the area along with plenty of moisture produced heat index values between 115 and 125 degrees. Surface Based CAPE values reached as high as 6000-6500 j/kg with DCAPE values approaching 1500-1600 j/kg. Thunderstorms initially formed in close proximity to an inland trough of low pressure and along the sea breeze. The convection sent out numerous outflow boundaries triggering additional thunderstorm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat index value of 118 degrees was measured by a mesonet station in Elim, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level short wave was moving through the Ohio and Tennessee Valley around a broad upper ridge centered across Texas.   A cold front was moving slowly south through the Ohio Valley and mid-Atlantic region.  With a warm, humid, unstable air mass remaining across Georgia, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours.  Thunderstorms were most numerous across north and west Georgia where the upper-level support was slightly better.  Several of the storms became severe and produced damaging downburst winds.  A couple of the storms also produced large golf ball-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter-sized hail near Cartersville ranging up to golf ball-sized hail near and just south of Emerson near the Cobb county bordern.  Penny-sized hail was also observed by the public just before this time frame in the White area northeast of Cartersville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid, unstable air mass remained across the southeastern United States.  Despite strong high pressure aloft centered over the southeastern U.S., scattered, diurnal pulse thunderstorms developed once again across the entire area during afternoon heating and continued well into the evening.  The thunderstorms that did develop dissipated quickly.  However, with surface temperatures in the mid to upper 90s, as the thunderstorms collapsed they produced damaging downburst winds.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was noted with many of the thunderstorms.  Yet another lightning injury occurred from these afternoon thunderstorms, continuing a very dangerous summer from lightning-related incidents across north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Douglas County 911 Center reported that three trees were down in the county from thunderstorm winds.  The trees were down on Wallace Lake Road, Beaver Lake Road, and Mann Road at the railroad crossing.  The tree down on Wallace Lake Road was also on a power line.  The public measured wind gusts at 43 mph during this thunderstorm in the Douglasville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid, unstable air mass remained across the southeastern United States.  Despite strong high pressure aloft centered over the southeastern U.S., scattered, diurnal pulse thunderstorms developed once again across the entire area during afternoon heating and continued well into the evening.  The thunderstorms that did develop dissipated quickly.  However, with surface temperatures in the mid to upper 90s, as the thunderstorms collapsed they produced damaging downburst winds.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was noted with many of the thunderstorms.  Yet another lightning injury occurred from these afternoon thunderstorms, continuing a very dangerous summer from lightning-related incidents across north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Emergency Management Director reported that approximately six trees and a couple of power lines were down around Wrightsville, especially on the north and west side of the city.  Trees were down on Oilver Road southwest of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across north and northeast Georgia while another stronger front approached from the Ohio Valley.  The subtropical ridge was building northward across the southern plains and mid-south.  A typical hot, humid, and unstable air mass remained in place across Georgia.  A complex of thunderstorms had moved across north Georgia during the late morning and early afternoon, leaving a significant cool outflow boundary draped near the Interstate-20 corridor by early afternoon. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms develop along and south of this outflow boundary during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many of these storms produced very heavy rain and frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  While none of the storms reached severe limits, one storm in Newnan blew down a tree, which caused damage to a Coweta county school building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockdale Citizen of Conyers reported that a home on Costly Mill Road was struck and set on fire by lightning.  The home was completely destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm became quite intense as it drifted southward, out of the mountains of extreme Northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down on Deer Path Ln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough of low pressure over the region in combination with numerous differential heating boundaries and the inland pushing sea breeze, resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and power lines down along Bloomingdale Road near Interstate 16, three miles south-southwest of Bloomingdale, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the inland pushing sea breeze and outflow boundaries from convection earlier in the day, resulted in a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported multiple large tree limbs down across Route 67 near Floyd Road, 3 miles northwest of Pembroke, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance in combination with the sea breeze in an unstable environment resulted in numerous thunderstorms developing across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bulloch county Emergency Management reported numerous trees downed by straight line winds, one of which fell on a home resulting in a moderate amount of roof damage. Winds also destroyed a storage shed.  The damage occurred on R.T. Myers Road and T.R. Smith Road, 2 miles east of Bay, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad and strong subtropical upper ridge was in place the mid-south into Florida.  The center of the ridge across Alabama and the Florida Panhandle provided Georgia with an unstable northwest flow aloft.  A weak backdoor front was evident across northeast Georgia with surface high pressure nosing southwest into Georgia.  Isolated storms developed on the east and southeast side of Atlanta during the mid-afternoon and propagated southwest onto the south side of Atlanta during the late afternoon before dissipating.  Nickel to quarter-sized hail, downburst winds, and frequent cloud-to-ground lightning were noted with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed nickel to quarter-sized hail east of Fayetteville near the Clayton county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic tornadic spring storm system was moving from the southern plains toward the Midwest and Ohio Valley as an intense, negatively tilted upper low and associated deep surface low lifted northeast from the Arklatex region toward the Ohio Valley.  The Storm Prediction Center had indicated a high risk of severe thunderstorms and tornadoes across western Tennessee, northern and central Alabama, and northern Mississippi.  A moderate risk of severe thunderstorms and tornadoes was projected into north central and northwest Georgia as well.  Deadly tornadoes had already occurred in northern Mississippi with this event.  However, a weak wedge of cooler, most stable air remained in place across much of north and east Georgia thanks to persistent showers and thunderstorms during the morning hours of the 24th.  A few of these morning and afternoon storms produced marginal severe hail.  Toward midnight, a long lived supercell tracked across north Alabama and into northwest Georgia before finally diminishing across western Fannin county.  This storm spawned several tornadoes along its path, including an EF1 tornado that tracked across the Alabama/Georgia border from DeKalb county in Alabama into Chattooga county, Georgia.  Fortunately, this was the only tornado observed in Georgia with this major spring storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Offices in both Huntsville, Alabama and Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that an EF1 tornado, which initially touched down in eastern DeKalb county Alabama approximately three miles southeast of Valley Head, Alabama, continued into Chattooga county Georgia, crossing into Georgia approximately two miles northwest of Cloudland in northwest Chattooga county.  The tornado continued on a nearly 10 mile long path within Chattooga county ending approximately 2.5 miles northwest of Trion in north central Chattooga county.  The tornado was rated an EF1 with a maximum path width of 300 yards and maximum wind gusts estimated to be 105 mph.  Considerable damage was noted along the tornado path across far northwest and north central Chattooga county.  At the touchdown point, at least 15 large trees were down across a one-half mile wide area.  As the tornado continued eastward, it crossed Georgia Highway 157 at Gilreath Mill Road.  Hunderds of large mature trees were down near the intersection.  Just east of this location, the tornado strengthened to its maximum width and intensity.  This was determined to be the mid-point of the tornado path, between Georgia Highway 337 and Harrisburg Road.  Within this area, eight homes sustained significant damage.  Two garages, one carport, and several private storage buildings were destroyed.  At a small grass airport strip, one hanger was destroyed, another suffered extensive damage, and two airplanes were flipped over.  One of the airplanes was an 800 lb ultralight and the other was a 1500 lb Cessna.  Nearby, a home lost its roof, another suffered extensive shingle damage, and another storage building was destroyed.  At the location of this destroyed outbuilding, two lawn mowers were moved 90 feet.  From this point to the end of the tornado path, hundreds of large, mature oak, pecan, and pine trees were blown down or uprooted.  Center Post Road, near the end point of the tornado path, was blocked because of numerous downed trees.  Damage to homes, businesses, the airport, and timber land, along with cleanup, was estimated to be $1.2 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A full-latitude trough was deepening across the eastern U.S., while also taking on an increasingly negative tilt.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper trough.  Considerable shear and dynamics were noted with the system.  While several tornado warnings were issued and a number of at least moderate circulations were evident on Doppler radar with some of the storms during this event, large hail was clearly the dominant severe weather type with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of quarter-sized hail were received from the public across the central part of the county from Rocky Face to Dawnville.  An amateur radio operator reported a heavy accumulation of hail along U.S. Highway 76 on the north side of Dalton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A full-latitude trough was deepening across the eastern U.S., while also taking on an increasingly negative tilt.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper trough.  Considerable shear and dynamics were noted with the system.  While several tornado warnings were issued and a number of at least moderate circulations were evident on Doppler radar with some of the storms during this event, large hail was clearly the dominant severe weather type with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Putnam County 911 Center reported golf ball-sized hail approximately six miles southeast of Eatonton.  The public observed quarter-sized hail on the Putnam county sided of Reynolds Plantation on Lake Oconee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening surface front was near southeast Georgia with a surface ridge axis across central Florida. A mean 1000-700 mb ridge axis was over southeast Georgia with a light south to southwest steering flow over the local area. An unstable low level air mass was in place and combined with the sea breezes and outflows numerous severe storms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down at the east end of Mush Bluff Road and just west of Crooked River State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was present over the Southeast. A weak lee-side trough developed over the Midlands during the afternoon and spawned showers and thunderstorms along residual convective outflows from the previous day's thunderstorm activity. Thunderstorms then shifted over portions of southeast Georgia during the early evening hours. An inland moving sea breeze prolonged shower and thunderstorms through the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported numerous roads not passable due to flooding near the intersections of Victory and Bull Street, Victory and Martin Luther King Street, Edgelawn and Pennsylvania Street, Tennessee and Pennsylvania Street and Gwinnett and Pennsylvania Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rumbled across the CSRA producing more wet microburst that took down trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP reported trees down in Appling, one was on a home that did moderate damage to it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable air mass resulted in the development of afternoon thunderstorms that produced some severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down along Highway 319 and along 5 Bridge Road near Ocilla.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over the Midlands of South Carolina during the afternoon hours. West steering flow along with a weak mid level shortwave brought a few of these showers and thunderstorms over portions of southeast Georgia during the evening hours. Thunderstorms displayed marginal hail and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 center reported a lightning strike very close to Fraser Circle where a 9 year old boy was outside. The boy was treated for lightning related injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching the region in combination with the sea breeze in a moist and unstable atmosphere, resulted in scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down at the intersection of East 31st Street and Cedar Street in Savannah, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours in association with an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although the official runway wind sensor was inoperative, the backup sensor measured a wind gust of 76 mph.  The observer on duty said that it was one of the worst storms he has seen.  The tower observers estimated winds near 70 mph.  Pilots in an A-10 aircraft reported severe shaking but no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong cold front was approaching Georgia from the northwest as the subtropical ridge retrograded west into Texas.  A disturbance in the increasingly stronger northwest flow aloft combined with the unstable, hot surface air mass to support the development of widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Many of the thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts downing numerous trees and power lines, especially across north Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported that two large pine trees were snapped, numerous large branches were down, and more than 50 percent of the shingles were peeled from a home's roof.  The spotter estimated that wind gusts were 60 mph or greater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While a strong subtropical ridge persisted across most of the southern United States, the remnants of Tropical Depression Number Five (TD5) continued to circulated around the subtropical ridge.  With the ridge at this time centered near New Orleans, the remnants of tropical depression number five had once again moved into Georgia.  Meanwhile...a weak frontal system had stalled across north Georgia, being pushed southward by a short wave moving through the Ohio Valley.  The combination of the front the remnants of TD5 resulted in numerous thunderstorms.  While none of the thunderstorms became severe, frequent lightning and very heavy rain accompanied many of the storms.  Localized flash flooding was reported in Cherokee county in north central Georgia during this event.  By far, however, the biggest issue on this day was frequent and intense cloud-to-ground lightning, which caused a rash of house fires in several northeast and east central Georgia counties.  The thunderstorms persisted into the early morning hours of the 21st across northeast Georgia between Athens, Lawrenceville, and Commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck a home on Woods River Lane in Duluth.  Firefighters were able to confine the fire to one wall, keeping damage to a minimum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge persisted across Texas, but was beginning to build wastward into the mid-south and southeast U.S.  An upper trough was located across the northeast into the mid-Atlantic region.  This provided Georgia with unstable northwest flow aloft.  Meanwhile...a weak cold front was moving slowly south through central Georgia.  Hot temperatures and dewpoints in the low and mid 70s made for a very unstable atmosphere across the region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the mid-afternoon and persisted into the evening.  The most persistent and intense thunderstorms originated from a cluster of thunderstorms that developed in northeast Georgia and slowly worked its way south-southwest into central Georgia.  Intense lightning and damaging downburst winds were noted with some of the thunderstorms, along with very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County Emergency Management Director reported that a portion of a roof was blown off a home near Arabi and a barn, approximately three miles east of Georgia Veterans Memorial State Park, lost its roof in strong thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The eastern U.S. upper trough noted on the 26th of September continued to evolve into a deep upper low across the southeast U.S. with an associated pocket of cold temperatures aloft.  Temperatures aloft at 500mb were near -20C during the late afternoon and early evening hours of September 27th.  As the preceding area of rain and thunderstorms exited the area to the east during the afternoon, cloudy conditions across north and west Georgia gave way to partly sunny skies.  This allowed temperatures to warm into the low and mid 80s across the region.  The warm surface temperatures created a very unstable environment as the upper low and cold core pocket rotated across the region.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across north Georgia, with lesser coverage of convective activity across central Georgia.  Only the southwest part of the county warning area managed to escape the afternoon thunderstorms.  The thunderstorms were fast moving, but several produced damaging downburst winds and penny to occasionally golf ball-sized hail.  A strong shower on the tail end of the convective activity (no lightning observed) even produced a brief tornado in Laurens county in southeast Georgia.  The severe weather event only lasted a couple of hours in the 530 pm to 730 pm time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens County Emergency Management Director reported that a weak and brief EF0 tornado touched down between Evergreen and Southern Belle Roads, just west of U.S. Highway 441, about 10 miles northwest of Dublin.  The tornado path length was estimated to only be 200 yards long with a maximum path width of 50 yards.  Twelve to 15 trees were down along the path of the tornado.  One of the downed trees destroyed an outbuilding and another caused minor damage to a home.  Radar data showed only a 40 dBZ shower in the area at the time on a tail end of much stronger activity across north central and east central Georgia.  No cloud-to-ground lightning was ever indicated in the shower that produced the tornado and the maximum cloud top on this cloud was below 20,000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper type system was moving quickly through the east and southeast U.S. bringing a surge of unusually cold air into the region.  Moisture was quite limited in the wake of the strong storm system that had just passed through the area on the 25th.  However, even with the lack of surface moisture and instability, very strong dynamics and cold air aloft allowed showers and even a few thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening as the cold front and upper trough swept through the region.  The activity was characterized by very little lightning, but a very low freezing level and strong evaporative cooling allowed wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph and dime to penny-sized hail to accompany many of the showers and thunderstorms that passed through the area during the afternoon and evening hours.  Temperatures dropped nearly 20 degrees from the 70s into the 50s with the passage of the showers as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that strong wind gusts associated with a line of showers and isolated thunderstorms blew down at least a dozen trees across the county.  An apartment building and adjacent garage were heavily damaged when a large oak tree fell on the structures. A vehicle was also damaged from a large downed tree limb.  Some power lines were also impacted by the downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough over the southeast Atlantic seaboard shifted south with moisture increasing over the area. A few strong to severe storms developed in the vicinity of the surface trough during the afternoon when instability was high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at a residence which damaged the car and caused a power outage at the home. The time of the event was based on radar. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough lingered just off the east coast of the United States.  This trough extended southwest into the southeast United States.  The southwest lagging part of this trough became detached from the mean flow and cutoff into a weak upper low across Alabama between May 29th and May 30th.  The combination of deep moisture, diurnal instability, and the upper low just to the west of Georgia provided ample support for scattered to numerous afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms. With the upper low immediately to the west, mid-level southeast flow resulted in a general southeast to northwest movement of the showers and thunderstorms.  Although a few of the storms approached severe limits, no thunderstorms meeting severe criteria were reported.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning and heavy rain were observed with many of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Muscogee County 911 Center reported that lightning struck a home in the Columbus area.  No specific information pertaining to damages was provided.  In addition, lightning took out power to several traffic signals in the city of Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level short wave was moving through the Ohio and Tennessee Valley around a broad upper ridge centered across Texas.   A cold front was moving slowly south through the Ohio Valley and mid-Atlantic region.  With a warm, humid, unstable air mass remaining across Georgia, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours.  Thunderstorms were most numerous across north and west Georgia where the upper-level support was slightly better.  Several of the storms became severe and produced damaging downburst winds.  A couple of the storms also produced large golf ball-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSB Television of Atlanta and the Douglas County 911 Center reported that several trees were down across the eastern half of the county.  Several trees were down along or near Georgia Highway 5, Thornton Road, and Sam Alexander road in Douglasville  Some trees were on homes and power lines.  Power lines were also down in Lithia Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across north and northeast Georgia while another stronger front approached from the Ohio Valley.  The subtropical ridge was building northward across the southern plains and mid-south.  A typical hot, humid, and unstable air mass remained in place across Georgia.  A complex of thunderstorms had moved across north Georgia during the late morning and early afternoon, leaving a significant cool outflow boundary draped near the Interstate-20 corridor by early afternoon. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms develop along and south of this outflow boundary during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many of these storms produced very heavy rain and frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  While none of the storms reached severe limits, one storm in Newnan blew down a tree, which caused damage to a Coweta county school building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Newnan Times-Herald reported that strong thunderstorm wind gusts blew down a large 100-year old oak tree onto the right front corner of a Coweta county school building near Newnan.  The downed tree caused damage to the metal roof, guttering, and minor damage to the conrete roof support underneath.  The Newnan fire department reported that they received over two dozen calls of downed trees and power lines around Newnan from the afternoon storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed upper low was moving southeast from the middle Tennessee Valley into the southeast United States.  An associated frontal system and low pressure area was moving from the eastern Gulf coastal region northeastward along the southeastern U.S. seaboard.  An unusually warm, moist atmosphere was present across the southeast U.S. in advance of this weather system.  Although overall instability was marginal, strong shear was present with this weather system.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia throughout the day.  While most of the storms were non-severe, one storm in northwest Georgia produced a significant downburst.  The storm simultaneously produced large hail, which when combined with the downburst winds produced a significant path of damage across Whitfield county.  Another storm during the evening spawned a brief EF0 tornado in the central Georgia county of Dooly, with very minimal damage noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The former Emergency Management Director for Oconee County observed penny to nickel-sized hail southwest of Bogart near the intersection of U.S. Highway 78 and Georgia Highway 53.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An weak upper trough continued to move from the southern plains toward the mid-Atlantic region.  A very warm and unstable air mass remained in place across the southeast United States.  Afternoon temperatures in the middle to upper 90s and deep moisture created a particularly unstable atmosphere across Georgia.  Convective Available Potential Energy (CAPE) values rose to in excess of 3000 J/KG during the afternoon.  Consequently, scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed, many of which became strong to severe.  Damaging downburst winds were noted with many of the storms.  As the storms moved into east Georgia, several of the storms produced quarter to golf ball-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter-sized hail in southeast Hall county near the Jackson county line.  Time estimated by radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough across the Ohio and Tennessee Valley was dropping south into the southeast U.S. and rapidly trending toward a deep, cold core upper low.  The first wave of inclement weather associated with this system occurred as an area of widespread moderate to heavy rain, with a line of strong thunderstorms along the leading edge.  While none of these thunderstorms became severe on the 26th, tornado watches were in effect for much of north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening and some storms along the leading edge of activity exhibited at least weak mesocyclones across east central Georgia.  A tornado warning was issued for a storm in southern Wilkes county during the late afternoon.  However, no tornado developed with this storm.  Widespread rainfall of two to three inches was common across east and south Georgia, with rainfall in excess of four inches in parts of Oglethorpe county.  Some flooding was observed in the Warner Robins area during the evening where rainfall of three to four inches was common.  This was the first widespread rain for the area since late August, so flooding in general was not an issue, despite the heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County Emergency Management Director reported that several roads from Warner Robins to Perry were flooded with several inches of water.  At least two roads had to be closed.  Two homes in Warner Robins were threatened by high water and were evacuated as a precaution.  Two retention ponds were found overflowing.  One home suffered minor flood damage in addition to debris removal from the flooded roads.

Temperatures aloft at 500mb were near -20C during the late afternoon and early evening hours of September 27th. The severe weather event only lasted a couple of hours in the 530 pm to 730 pm time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail in the Sandy Springs area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convergence within an area featuring mixed layer CAPES between 2500 to 3500 J/kg produced numerous pulse severe thunderstorms south of Interstate 16. A few thunderstorms became severe with quarter size hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees uprooted and powerlines down. A few trees fell on cabins and out buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tail end of a late spring season trough and associated cold front moved through the area and produced severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were reported down on Hickory Head Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through the CSRA and took down some trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported flooding at apartment complex with up to a foot of water in several apartments.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad and strong subtropical upper ridge was in place the mid-south into Florida.  The center of the ridge across Alabama and the Florida Panhandle provided Georgia with an unstable northwest flow aloft.  A weak backdoor front was evident across northeast Georgia with surface high pressure nosing southwest into Georgia.  Isolated storms developed on the east and southeast side of Atlanta during the mid-afternoon and propagated southwest onto the south side of Atlanta during the late afternoon before dissipating.  Nickel to quarter-sized hail, downburst winds, and frequent cloud-to-ground lightning were noted with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator observed quarter-sized hail in southern Clayton county between Fayetteville and Hampton, or just west of Lovejoy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical ridge that had been over the area for several days, was beginning to break down and shift east and south of the area.  This was allowing a cold front to approach from the north.  In addition, an subtropical low was evident across Texas and this was slowing drifting northeast.  A hot, unstable, and very moist tropical atmosphere remained across the region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed quickly during the afternoon across north Georgia, generally from Rome, to Canton, to Gainesville and northward.  The added synoptic support in comparison to previous days resulted in an upswing in the intensity and coverage of storms across this area.  Many of the storms became strong and a few became severe producing damaging downburst winds and large hail.  All of the storms produced frequent, intense cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daily Citizen of Dalton reported that a home on Robert Fuller Road in Tunnel Hill was struck by lightning.  The structure only sustained minimal damage from the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a weak frontal boundary aided by a weak shortwave trough, and advanced eastward into an unstable and weakly sheared environment over southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down on Islands Highway near Kings Road, 1 mile north-northwest of Halfmoon Landing, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms sparked a lightning fire on the Kings Bay Submarine Base. The fire burned about 54 acres by the time response crews had it contained by 2 a.m. LST Tuesday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Waycross Journal/Herald reported a lightning fire on the Kings Bay Submarine Base on July 12th. The time of the event was estimated as well as the cost of property damage. The fire was contained by 2 a.m. Tuesday morning.
